eco_task: Plan and Manage Scope
topic: Plan and Manage Scope
prompts: A project manager meets with the customer for a new application build to gather needs. The customer provides a single list without separating business needs from technical specifications. Which item on the list should be classified as a customer requirement?
During elicitation for a new app, the customer gives one mixed list of expectations and technical details. Which statement is a customer requirement?
A client provides requirements for a software product but does not distinguish user needs from technical specifications. Which item should the project manager classify as a customer requirement?
While gathering needs for an application, the project manager must separate customer requirements from implementation details. Which statement belongs with customer requirements?

correct.text: Users expect the application to be easy to use and navigate.
correct.why: It states a stakeholder need in user terms, not a technical specification.

correct.text: Users want the application to be simple to use and navigate.
correct.why: Customer requirements describe desired user outcomes rather than design details.

correct.text: Users need an application that feels easy to learn and move through.
correct.why: This expresses the customer's experience need without prescribing the solution.

distractors.text: Users expect the application to process 1,000 transactions per second.
distractors.trap: This is a measurable performance specification.

distractors.text: Users expect the application to use a secure database encryption algorithm.
distractors.trap: This prescribes a technical implementation.

distractors.text: Users expect the application to run on several operating systems.
distractors.trap: This is a compatibility specification.

distractors.text: Users expect the application to use a cloud-native container architecture.
distractors.trap: This specifies a technical design choice.

distractors.text: Users expect response times below a defined service threshold.
distractors.trap: This is a quantified nonfunctional requirement.

distractors.text: Users expect the application to integrate through a REST API.
distractors.trap: This names an implementation interface.

explanation: Customer requirements express what stakeholders want in general, business-oriented terms; the team later translates them into specific, testable technical requirements. Ease of use and navigation is a customer requirement.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Scope
topic: Plan and Manage Scope
prompts: During acceptance testing of a deliverable, senior stakeholders complain that a feature essential to their department was never included. What should the project manager have done up front to prevent this?
In user acceptance testing, executives discover that a capability critical to their department was omitted. What planning action should have prevented the gap?
A deliverable reaches acceptance review, and key stakeholders say an important departmental feature was never captured. What should the project manager have done earlier?
Late validation reveals that an essential stakeholder need was missing from scope. Which up-front action would have best prevented this?

correct.text: Analyzed the project charter and developed a scope management plan.
correct.why: Scope planning defines how requirements will be collected, documented, and validated.

correct.text: Use the charter to create a plan for managing project scope.
correct.why: A scope management plan guides complete requirement collection and validation.

correct.text: Develop a scope management plan based on the project charter.
correct.why: The plan establishes how stakeholder needs are captured early.

distractors.text: Consult lessons learned from similar projects to adapt processes.
distractors.trap: Lessons learned do not capture current needs.

distractors.text: Prepare a change management plan and log implementation changes.
distractors.trap: Change control does not prevent omitted requirements.

distractors.text: Prepare a prototype and conduct a product demonstration.
distractors.trap: A demo follows, not replaces, scope planning.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to approve missing features during acceptance testing.
distractors.trap: Late approval does not prevent the omission.

distractors.text: Increase contingency reserves for stakeholder-requested changes.
distractors.trap: Budget buffers do not capture scope.

distractors.text: Assign a senior developer to interview the affected department.
distractors.trap: Interviews need an overall scope process.

explanation: A missing requirement signals inadequate scope definition. Starting from the charter and building a scope management plan ensures all stakeholder needs are collected and validated up front.

eco_task: Integrate Project Planning Activities
topic: Integrate Project Planning Activities
prompts: Having finished the project management plan and confirmed it with key stakeholders, a project manager is now moving into the executing phase. What is the most appropriate next step?
The project management plan has been completed and aligned with major stakeholders. As the project enters execution, what should the project manager do next?
After planning is approved, the project manager transitions the team into executing. Which action is most appropriate at this point?
A project manager has stakeholder agreement on the management plan and is starting execution. What should happen next?

correct.text: Put the project plan into action.
correct.why: Execution is where planned work is directed and deliverables are produced.

correct.text: Begin carrying out the approved project plan.
correct.why: The executing phase implements the plan through project work.

correct.text: Direct the team to perform the planned project work.
correct.why: Executing focuses on completing the work defined in the plan.

distractors.text: Define the project and create the project charter.
distractors.trap: Chartering belongs to initiating.

distractors.text: Confirm that all deliverables are completed and accepted.
distractors.trap: Acceptance confirmation is closing.

distractors.text: Address risks to keep the project on track.
distractors.trap: Risk control is not the execution start.

distractors.text: Reopen planning to rewrite the management plan.
distractors.trap: The plan is already confirmed.

distractors.text: Archive project records and release project resources.
distractors.trap: Archiving and release occur at close.

distractors.text: Obtain authorization to formally start the project.
distractors.trap: Authorization precedes detailed planning.

explanation: With planning complete, the next phase is executing: the project manager and team carry out the plan by assigning work, engaging stakeholders, and producing deliverables.

eco_task: Manage Project Issues
topic: Manage Project Issues
prompts: During a project review, the construction manager notes that the concrete production rate has shifted over the past three months and wants an idea of what to expect over the next three. Which analysis should be conducted?
A manufacturing lead reviews three months of output data and wants to forecast likely production levels for the next three months. Which analysis is appropriate?
During performance review, the team sees that installation rates have changed over recent months and wants to predict future rates. What analysis should they use?
A project manager has historical productivity data over time and needs a forward-looking estimate for the same work. Which technique fits best?

correct.text: Trend analysis
correct.why: Trend analysis uses performance data over time to forecast future results.

correct.text: Perform trend analysis
correct.why: It examines patterns in past results to predict likely future performance.

correct.text: Use trend analysis
correct.why: Time-based performance patterns support forecasting through trend analysis.

distractors.text: Earned value analysis
distractors.trap: EVA assesses cost and schedule performance.

distractors.text: Critical path analysis
distractors.trap: Critical path identifies schedule sequence constraints.

distractors.text: Cost-benefit analysis
distractors.trap: CBA compares costs with benefits.

distractors.text: Root cause analysis
distractors.trap: Root cause analysis explains why issues occurred.

distractors.text: Monte Carlo analysis
distractors.trap: Monte Carlo models probability ranges.

distractors.text: Stakeholder analysis
distractors.trap: Stakeholder analysis assesses interests and influence.

explanation: Trend analysis uses historical data to project future performance, making it the right technique for forecasting the concrete production rate over coming months.

eco_task: Integrate Project Planning Activities
topic: Integrate Project Planning Activities
prompts: In a matrix organization, a project manager observes that the team is not working well together, is performing poorly, and is delivering low-quality results. How should the project manager first address this with the team?
A matrixed project team shows poor collaboration, weak performance, and quality problems. What should the project manager do first?
In a functional-matrix environment, team members are not working effectively together and deliverables are suffering. How should the project manager begin addressing the issue?
A project manager notices team friction, missed expectations, and low-quality outputs. What is the best first action with the team?

correct.text: Conduct a team meeting to discuss the issue.
correct.why: A group discussion surfaces causes and lets the team shape a shared solution.

correct.text: Bring the team together to discuss the performance problem.
correct.why: The issue involves team dynamics, so it should be addressed collectively first.

correct.text: Facilitate a team discussion about the collaboration and quality issues.
correct.why: Collective discussion helps identify the root cause of poor team performance.

distractors.text: Meet with each team member individually to gather perspectives.
distractors.trap: One-on-ones miss the group dynamic.

distractors.text: Create a communication plan for the specialists.
distractors.trap: A plan may treat symptoms only.

distractors.text: Reassign specialists to different roles immediately.
distractors.trap: Reassignment skips diagnosis.

distractors.text: Ask functional managers to correct their employees' behavior.
distractors.trap: This avoids team-level ownership.

distractors.text: Escalate the performance issue to the project sponsor.
distractors.trap: Escalation is premature before discussion.

distractors.text: Add more quality inspections to catch defects earlier.
distractors.trap: Inspections do not fix teamwork.

explanation: The first step is to gather the team to discuss the issue, identify the root cause, and collaboratively develop a plan. Individual talks, a communication plan, or reassignment do not resolve the underlying problem.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Schedule
topic: Plan and Manage Schedule
prompts: A newly assigned project manager is detailing every requirement and estimating each task needed to implement a healthcare system across three countries, then using that information to build the full project schedule. Which delivery approach is being used?
A project manager defines the full scope, estimates all tasks, and builds a complete schedule before implementation of a regional system rollout. What approach does this indicate?
For an enterprise implementation, the project manager documents all requirements and creates a detailed end-to-end schedule up front. Which delivery approach is this?
A team plans a large system deployment by specifying the complete scope and schedule before work begins. What life cycle approach is being used?

correct.text: A traditional delivery approach
correct.why: Detailed up-front scope and schedule planning indicate a predictive life cycle.

correct.text: A predictive traditional approach
correct.why: Traditional delivery plans the full scope and schedule before execution.

correct.text: Predictive traditional delivery
correct.why: Predictive approaches define requirements and schedule largely up front.

distractors.text: An agile delivery approach
distractors.trap: Agile plans and adapts iteratively.

distractors.text: A steel-thread delivery approach
distractors.trap: Steel thread delivers thin end-to-end increments.

distractors.text: A rolling-wave planning approach
distractors.trap: Rolling wave details near-term work first.

distractors.text: A Scrum delivery framework
distractors.trap: Scrum uses iterative sprint planning.

distractors.text: A Kanban flow-based approach
distractors.trap: Kanban manages flow, not full up-front planning.

distractors.text: An adaptive delivery approach
distractors.trap: Adaptive approaches expect evolving scope.

explanation: Defining detailed scope through a WBS and creating a complete schedule before execution characterizes a traditional, predictive project life cycle.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Schedule
topic: Plan and Manage Schedule
prompts: After a system review, a nursing team finds that integration of the Radiology Informatics System was missed and must go into the next sprint. The agile coach organizes a Planning Poker session to estimate the effort. Who is responsible for producing the estimate?
A missed integration item is added to the next sprint backlog, and the agile coach facilitates Planning Poker. Who should provide the effort estimate?
During sprint planning, the team needs to size newly discovered work. The coach sets up Planning Poker. Which group owns the estimate?
An agile team discovers overlooked integration work and uses Planning Poker to size it for the next sprint. Who creates the estimate?

correct.text: Project team
correct.why: In agile, the people doing the work estimate the effort.

correct.text: The delivery team
correct.why: Those responsible for delivery are best positioned to size the work.

correct.text: The agile project team
correct.why: Team-based estimation is a core agile practice.

distractors.text: Agile coach
distractors.trap: The coach facilitates, not estimates.

distractors.text: Value representative
distractors.trap: They prioritize value, not effort.

distractors.text: Stakeholders
distractors.trap: Stakeholders provide needs and priorities.

distractors.text: Project sponsor
distractors.trap: Sponsors authorize, not size work.

distractors.text: Functional manager
distractors.trap: Managers do not own agile estimates.

distractors.text: Product owner alone
distractors.trap: The product owner prioritizes backlog items.

explanation: In agile, work is estimated by those who will do it. The project team performing the increment makes the estimate, while the coach only facilitates.

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
prompts: At the end of a sprint the delivery team writes its lessons learned and states a clear willingness to improve their expertise through the retrospective. Based on the Agile Manifesto, what should the team focus on?
During a retrospective, team members document lessons learned and want to improve how they work together. Based on agile values, what should they emphasize?
At sprint close, the team reflects on its work and wants to strengthen expertise through the retrospective. What Agile Manifesto behavior best supports this?
A delivery team uses its retrospective to learn and improve. In line with the Agile Manifesto, which interaction style should they focus on?

correct.text: Conversing openly as a team
correct.why: Agile values individuals and interactions, so open conversation supports improvement.

correct.text: Open conversation during the retrospective
correct.why: Retrospectives rely on candid interaction to inspect and adapt.

correct.text: Collaborative conversation among team members
correct.why: Conversation reflects the Agile Manifesto's focus on people and interactions.

distractors.text: Competing for the strongest viewpoint
distractors.trap: Competition undermines collaboration.

distractors.text: Debating to win the discussion
distractors.trap: Debate can focus on winning arguments.

distractors.text: Defending individual past decisions
distractors.trap: Defensiveness blocks learning.

distractors.text: Escalating concerns outside the team
distractors.trap: Escalation is not a retrospective focus.

distractors.text: Directing the team toward answers
distractors.trap: Direction reduces team self-reflection.

distractors.text: Reporting status to management
distractors.trap: Reporting alone does not drive interaction.

explanation: The Agile Manifesto values individuals and interactions and collaboration. Conversation, over defending, debating, or competing, is how the team improves its expertise in a retrospective.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A new project manager joins a global team that must create a resource breakdown structure (RBS) in the coming days. However, the resource management plan states the RBS should be created and reviewed only at the start of the project. Which action should come first?
A project manager inherits a project where the team needs to update the RBS soon, but the resource management plan limits RBS creation and review to project start. What should be done first?
A distributed team must prepare a resource breakdown structure, yet the resource management plan says that activity happens only at project initiation. What is the first action?
The team needs an RBS for current planning, but the governing resource management plan restricts RBS work to the start of the project. How should the project manager proceed first?

correct.text: Revise the resource management plan.
correct.why: The plan contains the limiting rule and must be corrected before updating the RBS.

correct.text: Update the resource management plan first.
correct.why: The resource plan governs how the RBS is created and reviewed.

correct.text: Modify the resource management plan before changing the RBS.
correct.why: Correcting the governing plan enables the RBS update.

distractors.text: Revise the project management plan.
distractors.trap: The specific constraint is in the resource plan.

distractors.text: Revise the resource breakdown structure.
distractors.trap: The governing plan must change first.

distractors.text: Revise the work breakdown structure.
distractors.trap: The WBS decomposes scope, not resources.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to waive the resource plan requirement.
distractors.trap: Waiving skips proper plan control.

distractors.text: Create the RBS and document the exception later.
distractors.trap: This ignores the current plan.

distractors.text: Wait until the next project phase to review resources.
distractors.trap: Waiting does not meet the current need.

explanation: The RBS should be revisited throughout the project as resource needs change. Since the resource management plan wrongly limits this to project start, that plan must be revised first.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project is delayed because resource availability is limited, and the budget is now at risk. Which document should the project manager use to review and update the project's resources?

During a facilities upgrade, the schedule slips because several needed resources are unavailable, creating budget pressure. What artifact should the project manager review and update for the resources?

A nonprofit implementation project is falling behind after planned resources become constrained. The project manager needs to revisit the project's resource categories and types. Which document should be used?
On a healthcare project, resource availability is delaying work and threatening the budget. Which document helps the project manager review and revise the project's resources?

correct.text: Resource breakdown structure
correct.why: The RBS organizes resources by category and type for review as resource needs change.

correct.text: Resource breakdown structure (RBS)
correct.why: The RBS is the artifact used to categorize and update project resource information.

correct.text: The resource breakdown structure
correct.why: It supports reviewing resources by type and category when availability changes.

distractors.text: Requirements traceability matrix
distractors.trap: Tracks requirements to outputs, not resource categories.

distractors.text: Work breakdown structure
distractors.trap: Decomposes scope, not resource inventory.

distractors.text: Responsibility assignment matrix
distractors.trap: Maps assignments, not resource categories and types.

distractors.text: Stakeholder engagement plan
distractors.trap: Guides stakeholder interaction, not resource updates.

distractors.text: Issue log
distractors.trap: Records issues but does not structure resources.

distractors.text: Project schedule
distractors.trap: Shows timing, not resource classification details.

explanation: The resource breakdown structure is a hierarchical representation of resources by category and type and should be revisited throughout the project to adjust for changing resource needs.

eco_task: Integrate Project Planning Activities
topic: Integrate Project Planning Activities
prompts: A project manager overseeing a new product launch must ensure a Benefits Realization Plan is created per portfolio office guidelines and is documenting roles in a RACI chart. In that chart, who should be accountable for ensuring the project delivers value to the organization?
For a digital service rollout, the PMO requires a benefits realization plan, and the project manager is building a RACI chart. Which role should be accountable for organizational value delivery?

A project manager is assigning RACI roles for a strategic product initiative that includes benefits realization planning. Who should carry accountability for ensuring the project produces business value?
On a market expansion project, roles are being clarified in a RACI chart while the benefits realization plan is prepared. Which role is accountable for value delivery to the organization?

correct.text: Executive sponsor
correct.why: The sponsor owns alignment with strategic goals and accountability for business value.

correct.text: Project sponsor
correct.why: The sponsor is accountable for project success and value realization.

correct.text: The executive sponsor
correct.why: Sponsors are accountable for ensuring outcomes support organizational value.

distractors.text: Project manager
distractors.trap: Responsible for execution, not accountable for business value.

distractors.text: Solution architect
distractors.trap: Owns technical design, not value accountability.

distractors.text: Executive oversight panel
distractors.trap: Provides governance, not single accountable ownership.

distractors.text: Product development team
distractors.trap: Delivers work but does not own value accountability.

distractors.text: Portfolio analyst
distractors.trap: Supports analysis, not executive accountability.

distractors.text: Functional manager
distractors.trap: Supplies resources, not project value ownership.

explanation: The executive sponsor is the senior executive accountable for the project's success, ensuring it aligns with strategy and delivers expected business value, while others are responsible for execution or technical work.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: A project manager leads a business transformation at a company facing serious financial pressure from declining market share. Stakeholders doubt the need for change and are reluctant to invest. What should the project manager do to demonstrate the urgency and benefits of the change?
A retailer is losing share and facing financial strain. Leaders are skeptical about funding a transformation effort. What should the project manager prepare to show the need and benefits clearly?
In a manufacturing company under revenue pressure, stakeholders question whether a proposed change is worth the investment. How should the project manager demonstrate urgency and expected value?
A bank is considering an operating model change while market share falls. Several executives resist spending more. What action best shows why the change is urgent and beneficial?

correct.text: Create a business case quantifying benefits and linking them to company goals.
correct.why: A business case provides evidence and value rationale for investment decisions.

correct.text: Develop a business case that measures benefits against strategic objectives.
correct.why: Quantified benefits tied to strategy help persuade skeptical stakeholders.

correct.text: Prepare a business case showing benefits, costs, and goal alignment.
correct.why: It frames urgency and value in terms decision makers can evaluate.

distractors.text: Create a communication plan describing goals, milestones, and benefits.
distractors.trap: Communicates information but lacks investment justification.

distractors.text: Delegate work packages and provide specialists with needed resources.
distractors.trap: Addresses execution, not stakeholder investment concerns.

distractors.text: Schedule recurring stakeholder meetings to answer questions and provide updates.
distractors.trap: Meetings alone do not quantify value.

distractors.text: Update the risk register with threats related to declining market share.
distractors.trap: Captures risks but does not justify the change.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to mandate support for the transformation initiative.
distractors.trap: Uses authority instead of evidence-based persuasion.

distractors.text: Build a detailed project schedule for all transformation activities.
distractors.trap: Shows timing, not the business value case.

explanation: A business case provides an evidence-based rationale, covering strategic alignment, risk, ROI, and feasibility, that best convinces skeptical stakeholders to invest in the transformation.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: A company is developing a breakthrough product in a highly competitive, rapidly evolving market. The project manager expects the definition of done (DoD) to shift over time. What should the project manager do to ensure successful delivery of value?
A technology team is building an innovative product while competitor expectations change quickly. Because the definition of done may evolve, what should the project manager put in place?
On a fast-moving product initiative, market feedback may change what qualifies as done. What should the project manager do to keep value delivery on track?
A startup is developing a new platform in a volatile market, and completion criteria are expected to change. Which approach best supports successful value delivery?

correct.text: Implement strong project tracking and reporting procedures.
correct.why: Tracking and reporting expose changes early so the team can adapt.

correct.text: Use robust tracking and reporting throughout the project.
correct.why: Continuous visibility helps manage evolving completion criteria.

correct.text: Establish reliable progress tracking and reporting routines.
correct.why: Frequent insight supports timely adjustment as the DoD changes.

distractors.text: Expand the timeline and budget to absorb possible risks.
distractors.trap: Padding does not address evolving completion criteria.

distractors.text: Add more resources to accelerate product development.
distractors.trap: More people may not improve value delivery.

distractors.text: Restrict scope and focus on fixed delivery dates.
distractors.trap: Rigid scope can undermine market value.

distractors.text: Freeze the definition of done at project kickoff.
distractors.trap: Ignores the expected need to adapt.

distractors.text: Move quality reviews to the end of the project.
distractors.trap: Late review reduces visibility and adaptability.

distractors.text: Escalate each criteria change to senior leadership.
distractors.trap: Creates delay instead of ongoing control.

explanation: In uncertain, fast-changing environments the DoD may shift. Robust tracking and reporting let the project manager monitor progress, spot issues, and adapt in time to keep delivering value.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: A project manager is running an Artificial Intelligence project involving hundreds of teams spread across multiple locations. Which model is best suited to customer focus, collaboration, and timely delivery at that scale?
A global AI program has hundreds of distributed teams that must collaborate around customer value and deliver quickly. Which delivery model best fits this scale?
A project manager needs a framework for a very large multi-site product effort with many teams, strong customer focus, and frequent collaboration. Which model should be selected?

An enterprise analytics initiative involves hundreds of teams in different regions. The organization wants customer focus, transparency, collaboration, and timely delivery. Which model is most appropriate?

correct.text: Large-Scale Scrum for many teams
correct.why: LeSS scales Scrum principles across many teams while preserving customer-centric collaboration.

correct.text: Large-Scale Scrum (LeSS)
correct.why: LeSS coordinates multiple teams around shared product value and transparency.

correct.text: LeSS for scaled Scrum delivery
correct.why: LeSS is designed to scale Scrum for many teams delivering one product.

distractors.text: Predictive delivery for the full program
distractors.trap: Too sequential for a large evolving effort.

distractors.text: Hybrid delivery without a scaling framework
distractors.trap: Generic hybrid lacks a specific scaling structure.

distractors.text: Lean Kanban for team workflow
distractors.trap: Optimizes flow but not hundreds of Scrum teams.

distractors.text: Waterfall delivery across all teams
distractors.trap: Emphasizes fixed phases, not customer collaboration at scale.

distractors.text: Critical Chain for schedule buffers
distractors.trap: Focuses on schedule buffers, not agile scaling.

distractors.text: Design thinking for discovery work
distractors.trap: Supports discovery, not delivery coordination at scale.

explanation: Large-Scale Scrum is designed to scale agile across many teams with greater customer focus and collaboration, emphasizing learning, transparency, and customer-centric value delivery.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: A major project deliverable has just been completed. What should the project manager and team do to confirm that the specifications were met and obtain formal acceptance?
A supplier portal deliverable is ready for review. Which process should the team use to confirm it meets requirements and secure formal acceptance?
After completing a key deliverable, the project manager needs stakeholder confirmation that it satisfies the agreed specifications. What should be done?
A construction project has finished an important work package. The project manager must verify the output with stakeholders and obtain acceptance. Which action fits?

correct.text: Validate the scope.
correct.why: Validate Scope confirms completed deliverables and obtains formal stakeholder acceptance.

correct.text: Perform Validate Scope.
correct.why: This process checks deliverables against requirements for formal acceptance.

correct.text: Conduct scope validation.
correct.why: Scope validation is used to accept completed deliverables formally.

distractors.text: Check the organizational process assets.
distractors.trap: OPAs do not accept deliverables.

distractors.text: Validate the quality audit reports.
distractors.trap: Audits evaluate processes, not acceptance.

distractors.text: Perform quality control.
distractors.trap: QC checks defects but does not obtain acceptance.

distractors.text: Update the lessons learned register.
distractors.trap: Captures knowledge, not formal acceptance.

distractors.text: Review the risk register.
distractors.trap: Risk review does not confirm specifications.

distractors.text: Issue a change request.
distractors.trap: A change request is unnecessary for acceptance.

explanation: Validate Scope examines the completed deliverable against the scope baseline and secures formal acceptance from stakeholders, confirming that specifications were met.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: On an application build, several specialists are so focused on individual features that they cannot agree on which to prioritize. A stakeholder contacts the project manager to raise concerns about value delivery. What action should the project manager take?
A software team is debating features from narrow technical viewpoints and cannot settle priority. A business stakeholder worries value may be missed. What should the project manager do?
During a product build, team members disagree about which features should come first because each favors their own area. How should the project manager respond to protect value delivery?
On a data platform project, specialists are prioritizing isolated features differently, and a stakeholder questions whether the project is delivering value. What is the best next action?

correct.text: Meet with the team to prioritize features based on value.
correct.why: Collaborative prioritization creates shared understanding and value-driven buy-in.

correct.text: Facilitate a team plan for value-based feature prioritization.
correct.why: The team should align priorities around delivered value.

correct.text: Work with the team to rank features by business value.
correct.why: Involving the team supports ownership of value-based decisions.

distractors.text: Ask the stakeholder for a checklist of the most important features.
distractors.trap: Bypasses team insight and constraints.

distractors.text: Mandate a prioritization scheme after obtaining approval.
distractors.trap: Imposes priorities without team buy-in.

distractors.text: Perform quality inspections and report results to the stakeholder.
distractors.trap: Addresses quality, not prioritization.

distractors.text: Escalate the disagreement to the sponsor for a decision.
distractors.trap: Skips facilitation and shared ownership.

distractors.text: Prioritize the technically simplest features first.
distractors.trap: Ease is not the same as value.

distractors.text: Delay prioritization until the next release planning cycle.
distractors.trap: Postpones the value delivery concern.

explanation: Meeting with the team to prioritize by value aligns work with stakeholder concerns and builds buy-in, since team involvement produces a plan that is both feasible and valuable.

eco_task: Manage Project Issues
topic: Manage Project Issues
prompts: A project manager identifies that work already started by the team is inconsistent with industry regulations. Which document should the project manager update to record this?
During execution, a project manager discovers that completed work does not comply with applicable regulations. Where should this be recorded for tracking?
A healthcare implementation team has begun work that conflicts with regulatory requirements. Which project artifact should the project manager update?
On a financial services project, the team is already performing work that violates an industry rule. What should the project manager update to log the matter?

correct.text: Issue tracker
correct.why: Regulatory noncompliance is an active issue that must be logged and tracked.

correct.text: Issue log
correct.why: An occurred compliance problem is managed as an issue.

correct.text: The issue tracker
correct.why: The issue tracker records problems requiring follow-up and resolution.

distractors.text: Organizational process assets
distractors.trap: Reference assets are not issue records.

distractors.text: Work performance data
distractors.trap: Raw measurements do not log issues.

distractors.text: Project management plan
distractors.trap: Contains plans, not active issue records.

distractors.text: Risk register
distractors.trap: The risk has occurred; it is now an issue.

distractors.text: Requirements traceability matrix
distractors.trap: Tracks requirements, not compliance issues.

distractors.text: Change log
distractors.trap: Records change requests, not every issue.

explanation: The issue tracker records and tracks issues that arise from monitoring and controlling activities. Noncompliance with industry regulations is an issue, so it belongs in the issue tracker.

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
prompts: You are helping your organization transition from predictive to agile methods. Leadership complains that less work is getting done since agile was introduced. How should you respond to the leadership team?
An operations group has just begun moving from a predictive approach to agile. Executives notice lower output and question the change. What should the project manager explain?
During an agile transformation, managers say teams are producing less than before and want an immediate explanation. How should the project manager respond?
A company is adopting agile practices after years of plan-driven delivery. Early productivity appears lower, and leadership is concerned. What is the best response?

correct.text: Explain that a temporary output dip is expected while teams learn agile.
correct.why: Agile adoption has a learning curve before benefits become visible.

correct.text: Clarify that short-term slowdown is normal during agile transition.
correct.why: Teams need time to adjust to new roles, cadence, and collaboration.

correct.text: Set expectations that productivity may dip while agile practices mature.
correct.why: Transformation disruption often precedes improved flow and value delivery.

distractors.text: Ask each team lead to justify why less work is being completed.
distractors.trap: Frames learning as a performance failure.

distractors.text: Require every team to use identical two-week sprints.
distractors.trap: Forces uniform cadence instead of adaptation.

distractors.text: Stop the agile rollout until output improves.
distractors.trap: Abandons the change during normal adjustment.

distractors.text: Return to predictive methods for all active projects.
distractors.trap: Overreacts before agile has stabilized.

distractors.text: Increase reporting requirements to prove teams are working.
distractors.trap: Adds overhead rather than supporting learning.

distractors.text: Replace team members who struggle with the new practices.
distractors.trap: Punishes transition challenges instead of coaching.

explanation: A drop in output early in an agile transition is expected as teams learn new practices; leadership should be reassured that benefits emerge after the adjustment period.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: You are reviewing project histories, resources, and performance data so you can apply stored organizational knowledge to a new project. Where should you look first?
A project manager wants historical resource data, past project performance, and lessons from completed work before starting a new initiative. Which source should be checked first?
Before planning a new project, you need access to current stored project histories and performance information. Where is the best place to look?
A team is preparing for a new implementation and wants to reuse organizational project knowledge, including histories and resource information. What should they consult first?

correct.text: The PMIS.
correct.why: The PMIS consolidates project information and historical data for reuse.

correct.text: Project management information system
correct.why: The PMIS is the primary source for stored project records.

correct.text: The project management information system (PMIS).
correct.why: It keeps project histories, resources, and performance information available.

distractors.text: Previously approved project charters
distractors.trap: Charters lack full histories and performance data.

distractors.text: Quality management tools
distractors.trap: Do not store broad project history.

distractors.text: Baseline-versus-actual performance reports
distractors.trap: Provide only part of the information.

distractors.text: Stakeholder engagement plans
distractors.trap: Focus on engagement, not historical knowledge.

distractors.text: Procurement bid documents
distractors.trap: Concern sourcing, not project knowledge reuse.

distractors.text: Team working agreements
distractors.trap: Define team norms, not enterprise history.

explanation: The project management information system organizes the full body of project information and is updated through completion, so it is the first place to find histories, resources, and performance data for reuse.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: During the implementation phase, a key stakeholder requests new features for the product, insisting they will add value. Which action should the project manager take first?
Midway through implementation, a senior stakeholder asks to add product capabilities because they may increase value. What should the project manager do first?
On a product project in execution, a business lead requests additional features and says they are valuable. Which first step is most appropriate?
A key stakeholder proposes new functionality during implementation. Before any decision is made, what should the project manager do?

correct.text: Assess the change's impact on project budget and schedule.
correct.why: Change requests must be evaluated for impact before approval or implementation.

correct.text: Evaluate how the requested features affect cost and timing.
correct.why: Impact analysis is required before deciding on a change.

correct.text: Analyze the budget and schedule impact of the requested change.
correct.why: The project manager should assess effects before taking action.

distractors.text: Meet with the sponsor to discuss the benefits of the features.
distractors.trap: Escalates before impact analysis.

distractors.text: Review the stakeholder engagement plan for involvement level.
distractors.trap: Does not evaluate the change.

distractors.text: Implement the change and document lessons learned.
distractors.trap: Implements before approval.

distractors.text: Add the features directly to the product backlog.
distractors.trap: Skips impact assessment and decision making.

distractors.text: Reject the request because implementation has already started.
distractors.trap: Dismisses a possible valuable change prematurely.

distractors.text: Ask the team to begin design work immediately.
distractors.trap: Starts work before evaluating the request.

explanation: Proposed changes must follow the change management plan, starting with assessing their impact on budget and schedule; a change must be evaluated and approved before it is implemented or escalated.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Scope
topic: Plan and Manage Scope
prompts: The sponsor of a hybrid project wants a more detailed breakdown in the project charter rather than just milestones. The project manager explains that preparing this breakdown would require two more iterations. Why is the extra time needed?
On a hybrid initiative, the sponsor asks the project manager to add more detail to the charter beyond milestone-level information. The PM says two more iterations are needed. Why?
A sponsor wants the project charter expanded into a detailed breakdown before planning is underway. The project manager says additional iterations are required. What is the reason?
During initiation of a hybrid project, the sponsor requests more detail in the charter than the current milestone view. Why would the PM need more time before detailed planning?

correct.text: The charter scope must be approved before detailed team planning begins.
correct.why: Detailed planning depends on approved high-level scope in the charter.

correct.text: Approved charter scope is needed before the team can plan in detail.
correct.why: The team should not develop detailed plans until scope is authorized.

correct.text: Detailed planning follows approval of the project scope in the charter.
correct.why: Scope approval provides the basis for the team's detailed planning.

distractors.text: The milestone schedule was based only on the project manager's experience.
distractors.trap: Addresses schedule basis, not planning sequence.

distractors.text: The oversight panel must approve the schedule before any planning.
distractors.trap: Adds an unsupported governance gate.

distractors.text: Hybrid projects plan schedule details only one iteration ahead.
distractors.trap: Misstates the reason for extra time.

distractors.text: The product owner must complete the backlog before charter approval.
distractors.trap: Backlog completion is not required here.

distractors.text: The project manager must create the WBS without team input.
distractors.trap: Detailed planning should involve the team.

distractors.text: The sponsor must provide all task estimates before initiation ends.
distractors.trap: Sponsors do not provide detailed task estimates.

explanation: A detailed breakdown requires detailed planning with the team, which can only proceed once the charter's scope is approved, and that approval and planning take the additional iterations.

eco_task: Manage Project Issues
topic: Manage Project Issues
prompts: You are managing an agile project with many risks in the risk register. The current sprint covers a critical part of the project. During the iteration, an identified risk occurs and halts development. You log the risk occurrence. What is the most appropriate next step?
In an agile project, a known risk from the register materializes during a critical sprint and stops development. After logging the occurrence, what should the project manager do next?
A key sprint is blocked when an identified risk becomes an actual issue. The project manager records it. Which next action best supports resolution?
During an iteration, development halts because a previously identified risk has occurred. Once it is logged, what should the project manager do?

correct.text: Increase communication frequency and involve stakeholders in resolving the issue.
correct.why: Blocked work needs immediate collaboration and stakeholder support to resolve.

correct.text: Communicate more often and enlist stakeholders to help remove the blocker.
correct.why: A halted sprint requires active issue resolution, not delayed reporting.

correct.text: Raise communication cadence and engage stakeholders to resolve it.
correct.why: The issue is stopping progress and needs coordinated action now.

distractors.text: Assign the communication management plan to a senior team member.
distractors.trap: Delegates planning instead of resolving the blocker.

distractors.text: Invite stakeholders to the next retrospective and brief them then.
distractors.trap: Waiting is too slow for halted work.

distractors.text: Defer the critical work and pull new backlog items into the sprint.
distractors.trap: Avoids the blocker and disrupts sprint commitment.

distractors.text: Update the risk register and wait for the next daily standup.
distractors.trap: Logging alone is insufficient when work is stopped.

distractors.text: Ask the product owner to lower the sprint goal.
distractors.trap: Reduces scope before attempting resolution.

distractors.text: Close the risk because it has already occurred.
distractors.trap: Occurrence creates an issue needing resolution.

explanation: When an issue halts progress, effective issue management calls for increased communication and pulling in stakeholders to help resolve it immediately, not waiting for the retrospective or reshuffling the active sprint.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Procurement
topic: Plan and Manage Procurement
prompts: You are leading a project with a vendor engaged under a fixed-price contract. Although the requirements and timelines were clearly defined and shared with the vendor, the executive sponsor is worried about a cost overrun. How should you respond to the sponsor?
A supplier is performing clearly defined work under a fixed-price agreement. The sponsor asks how the buyer should mitigate possible vendor cost overruns. What should the project manager explain?
On a project with agreed requirements, schedule, and a fixed-price vendor contract, leadership is concerned about rising vendor costs. How should the project manager respond?
A vendor has accepted a fixed price for well-defined deliverables and timelines. The executive sponsor worries costs may exceed expectations. What is the appropriate response?

correct.text: Explain that cost-overrun risk is transferred to the vendor.
correct.why: In fixed-price contracts, the seller generally bears cost-overrun risk.

correct.text: State that the vendor owns the overrun risk under the fixed price.
correct.why: A fixed-price agreement shifts cost risk from buyer to seller.

correct.text: Clarify that the fixed-price contract places cost risk on the seller.
correct.why: The vendor must deliver for the agreed price unless terms change.

distractors.text: Raise the concern at the next team meeting for feedback.
distractors.trap: Seeks input on a contract-handled risk.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to add contingency funding.
distractors.trap: Buyer reserves are not needed for vendor overruns.

distractors.text: Issue an RFP to find backup vendors.
distractors.trap: Re-procures without a buyer-side issue.

distractors.text: Convert the agreement to cost-reimbursable terms.
distractors.trap: Would shift more cost risk to the buyer.

distractors.text: Create a reserve for all vendor labor increases.
distractors.trap: Covers risk already shifted to the seller.

distractors.text: Escalate immediately to terminate the vendor.
distractors.trap: No breach or overrun has occurred.

explanation: In a fixed-price contract the vendor assumes the cost-overrun risk, so the PM can reassure the sponsor that no buyer-side mitigation, funding, or re-procurement is required.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: During your project's implementation phase, a stakeholder reports that sales have dropped sharply. You investigate and find an unforeseen shift in market conditions has cut demand for the product, reducing sales. What should you do first to address this?
While a product project is being implemented, a sales leader reports a steep decline. Analysis shows an unexpected market change has reduced demand. What should the project manager do first?
A stakeholder warns that demand has fallen during execution. The project manager confirms a market shift is reducing sales for the product. Which action comes first?
During implementation, new market conditions unexpectedly reduce customer demand and sales. What should the project manager do before forecasting or changing reserves?

correct.text: Identify risks from the market shift and plan responses.
correct.why: New risks must be understood before forecasts or reserves are adjusted.

correct.text: Determine the potential risks created by the market change.
correct.why: Risk identification is the first step after discovering the new condition.

correct.text: Identify and plan for risks arising from reduced demand.
correct.why: The project needs risk analysis before choosing corrective actions.

distractors.text: Use forecasting formulas to estimate demand impact.
distractors.trap: Forecasting should follow risk identification.

distractors.text: Increase the contingency reserve for response costs.
distractors.trap: Adjusts reserves before understanding risks.

distractors.text: Use earned value management to calculate EAC.
distractors.trap: EVM does not address market demand risk.

distractors.text: Request immediate scope reduction from the sponsor.
distractors.trap: Jumps to action before risk analysis.

distractors.text: Revise the sales target in the benefits plan.
distractors.trap: Changes targets before evaluating impacts.

distractors.text: Hold a lessons learned session with the team.
distractors.trap: Learning is premature while the risk is active.

explanation: Facing a sudden market change, the PM should first identify and analyze the resulting risks and plan responses; forecasting, reserve adjustments, and EVM only make sense once those risks are understood.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project is slightly ahead of schedule, but the project manager learns that a critical deliverable will not be ready on time. If the project manager crashes the deliverable that is behind, what would the impact be?
A project has some schedule float overall, but one critical deliverable is running late. What is the likely effect if the project manager crashes that deliverable?
On a facilities project, a key deliverable is behind even though the total project is slightly ahead. If the team uses crashing, what impact should be expected?
A project manager considers adding resources to compress the schedule for a late critical deliverable. What is the typical result of this crashing approach?

correct.text: Project costs and risks would increase.
correct.why: Crashing adds resources to compress schedule, increasing cost and risk.

correct.text: Costs and risks would rise.
correct.why: Schedule compression through crashing typically requires added resources and risk.

correct.text: The project would face higher cost and risk.
correct.why: Crashing trades schedule recovery for greater expense and uncertainty.

distractors.text: Project warranty issues would result.
distractors.trap: Warranty is unrelated to crashing.

distractors.text: Project costs and risks would decrease.
distractors.trap: Reverses the effect of crashing.

distractors.text: Rework would need to be performed.
distractors.trap: Confuses crashing with quality failure.

distractors.text: The critical path would be eliminated.
distractors.trap: Crashing does not remove critical path logic.

distractors.text: Scope would automatically be reduced.
distractors.trap: Crashing does not reduce scope.

distractors.text: Stakeholder engagement would improve.
distractors.trap: Not a direct crashing impact.

explanation: Crashing compresses the schedule by adding resources, which increases both project cost and risk.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: A project has outsourced components, and the project manager is in the control phase of the procurement process. What should the project manager focus on doing?
Several project deliverables are being provided by outside vendors. During Control Procurements, where should the project manager focus attention?
A project manager is managing active supplier contracts after vendors have been selected. Which activity fits the control phase of procurement?
On an outsourced implementation, procurement work is underway and must be controlled. What should the project manager do during this phase?

correct.text: Evaluate the performance of a major project vendor.
correct.why: Control Procurements monitors seller performance against contract requirements.

correct.text: Monitor and evaluate vendor performance.
correct.why: The control phase ensures suppliers meet agreed obligations.

correct.text: Review supplier performance against the contract.
correct.why: Control Procurements focuses on managing active procurement relationships.

distractors.text: Finalize the contract and pay the vendors.
distractors.trap: Belongs to procurement closure.

distractors.text: Create a procurement plan and identify potential vendors.
distractors.trap: Belongs to procurement planning.

distractors.text: Issue RFPs and select vendors.
distractors.trap: Belongs to conducting procurements.

distractors.text: Draft the make-or-buy analysis.
distractors.trap: Occurs before vendor control.

distractors.text: Develop source selection criteria.
distractors.trap: Used before suppliers are chosen.

distractors.text: Archive procurement documents for future projects.
distractors.trap: Closure activity, not control.

explanation: In Control Procurements the PM monitors vendor performance against agreed requirements and manages changes and issues; planning, selecting, and closing occur in other procurement phases.

eco_task: Manage Project Issues
topic: Manage Project Issues
prompts: A key team member is leaving the organization at the halfway point of the project. Which action should the project manager take first?
Midway through a project, a critical specialist gives notice that they are leaving the company. What should the project manager do first?
A project loses a senior engineer halfway through execution. Before replacing or reassigning work, what should the project manager do?
During implementation, an essential analyst announces they will leave the organization. Which first action is most appropriate for the project manager?

correct.text: Assess how the team member's loss will affect the project.
correct.why: Impact must be understood before choosing replacement or reassignment actions.

correct.text: Evaluate the project impact of losing the key team member.
correct.why: Assessment comes before deciding how to fill the gap.

correct.text: Determine the impact of the departure on the project.
correct.why: Sound resource decisions require knowing the effect first.

distractors.text: Meet with remaining team members to reassign the workload.
distractors.trap: Reassigns before understanding impact.

distractors.text: Work with the functional manager to find a replacement.
distractors.trap: Jumps to solution before assessment.

distractors.text: Reassess all resource roles and team composition.
distractors.trap: Restructures prematurely.

distractors.text: Escalate the issue immediately to the sponsor.
distractors.trap: Escalates before analyzing the effect.

distractors.text: Update the schedule baseline to reflect delays.
distractors.trap: Changes baseline before assessing impact.

distractors.text: Ask the departing member to train a replacement.
distractors.trap: Assumes the right response before analysis.

explanation: The first step is to understand how the departure affects the project by assessing the leaving member's responsibilities and expertise. Reassigning, replacing, or restructuring all come after that impact analysis.

eco_task: Manage Project Artifacts
topic: Manage Project Artifacts
prompts: A project manager new to Agile sees that a colleague's projects struggled because objectives and the definition of done were never clearly set, so sponsors kept adding features and the projects never finished. What should the project manager do to avoid these problems?
After seeing agile efforts fail from unclear goals and shifting completion criteria, a project manager wants to prevent endless added features. What should be done early?
A new agile project manager learns that prior teams struggled because sponsors kept expanding scope without clear objectives. Which action helps avoid the same issue?
On an agile initiative, the project manager wants clear boundaries before sponsors start adding features. What should the project manager do with key stakeholders?

correct.text: Meet with key stakeholders to build the Agile charter and set expectations.
correct.why: The charter aligns goals, boundaries, and expectations before work expands.

correct.text: Create an Agile project charter with key stakeholder input.
correct.why: Stakeholder alignment on objectives helps limit scope creep.

correct.text: Facilitate an Agile charter session to clarify objectives and expectations.
correct.why: Clear chartering reduces ambiguity about outcomes and completion.

distractors.text: Ensure every task is listed in the project management plan.
distractors.trap: Task detail does not clarify objectives.

distractors.text: Add quality experts so the product can be tested later.
distractors.trap: Late testing does not prevent unclear goals.

distractors.text: Ask the product owner to define objectives alone.
distractors.trap: Needs shared stakeholder alignment.

distractors.text: Freeze the backlog after the first sprint planning meeting.
distractors.trap: Ignores agile adaptation and alignment.

distractors.text: Escalate feature requests directly to the sponsor.
distractors.trap: Does not establish initial boundaries.

distractors.text: Schedule acceptance testing at the end of the project.
distractors.trap: Does not address unclear expectations up front.

explanation: Building an Agile project charter with key stakeholders sets shared goals, objectives, and a definition of done, which curbs scope creep and keeps the project on track.

eco_task: Manage Project Artifacts
topic: Manage Project Artifacts
prompts: A company hires a software vendor to build and customize a new application and assigns a project manager to initiate and lead the work. Which artifact describes the key deliverables the project will produce?
An organization contracts a vendor to configure a new application. As the project is initiated, which artifact states the major deliverables to be produced?
A project manager is starting work with a software supplier on a customized platform. What document describes the project's deliverables and acceptance criteria?
During initiation of a vendor-led application project, the team needs an artifact that defines what the project will produce. Which one is appropriate?

correct.text: Project scope statement
correct.why: It describes project deliverables, goals, and acceptance criteria.

correct.text: The project scope statement
correct.why: The scope statement defines what the project will produce.

correct.text: Project scope statement document
correct.why: It captures the key deliverables and boundaries of project work.

distractors.text: Business case
distractors.trap: Justifies value, not deliverables.

distractors.text: Agreements
distractors.trap: Define terms, not detailed deliverables.

distractors.text: Project schedule
distractors.trap: Shows timing, not produced outputs.

distractors.text: Benefits realization plan
distractors.trap: Tracks benefits, not deliverable definitions.

distractors.text: Stakeholder register
distractors.trap: Identifies people, not project outputs.

distractors.text: Resource management plan
distractors.trap: Plans resources, not deliverables.

explanation: The project scope statement details the scope, deliverables, and acceptance criteria, giving stakeholders a clear understanding of what the project will produce.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Schedule
topic: Plan and Manage Schedule
prompts: You are halfway through an Agile project of 100 backlog items split across five iterations. Each user story is worth US$51, the actual cost so far is US$2,007, and 50 backlog items have been delivered. Which statement is true?

correct.text: The project is under budget and on schedule.
correct.why: Earned value equals planned value (SPI = 1) while actual cost is lower, giving CPI above 1.

distractors.text: The project is over budget and behind schedule.
distractors.trap: Misreads both indices as unfavorable when SPI is 1 and CPI exceeds 1.

distractors.text: The project is on budget and on schedule.
distractors.trap: Ignores that actual cost is below earned value, so it is under budget.

distractors.text: The project is under budget and ahead of schedule.
distractors.trap: Confuses SPI of exactly 1 with being ahead of schedule.

explanation: At the halfway point, earned value equals planned value (SPI = 1, on schedule) while actual cost is below earned value (CPI greater than 1, under budget).

eco_task: Manage Project Artifacts
topic: Manage Project Artifacts
prompts: After drafting the project charter, the project manager sends it to the stakeholders. What should the project manager do before beginning the project planning process?
A project manager has prepared a charter for a new operations initiative and shared it with key stakeholders. Before moving into detailed planning, what must happen next?
For a new digital services project, the charter has been written and circulated for stakeholder review. What should the project manager secure before planning starts?
A project is still in initiation, and the project charter has just been drafted. Which action is required before the team begins project planning?

correct.text: Obtain approval of the project charter.
correct.why: The charter must be authorized before downstream planning begins.

correct.text: Secure formal authorization of the charter.
correct.why: Authorized initiation is required before planning work proceeds.

correct.text: Get the project charter approved.
correct.why: Approval confirms the project is authorized to move into planning.

distractors.text: Conduct the project's kickoff meeting.
distractors.trap: Kickoff typically follows planning, not charter drafting.

distractors.text: Create the project risk register.
distractors.trap: Risk identification is part of planning.

distractors.text: Develop the work breakdown structure.
distractors.trap: The WBS is created during planning.

distractors.text: Baseline the project schedule.
distractors.trap: Schedule baselining comes after planning.

distractors.text: Assign work packages to the team.
distractors.trap: Work assignment requires planning artifacts first.

distractors.text: Close the initiation phase retrospectively.
distractors.trap: Closure does not replace charter authorization.

explanation: The charter must be authorized before planning begins; if stakeholders change the scope, it would affect the WBS and risk register, and initiation would be incomplete.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: You are working with stakeholders to define the steps of a task and need to verify that team members have completed every required step of this frequently performed task. What should the project manager use?
A team performs the same operational task many times. Stakeholders have defined the required steps, and the project manager needs a way to confirm each step is done. Which tool fits best?
On a quality improvement project, team members repeat a standard procedure often. What should the project manager use to verify that no required step is missed?
A project manager needs a simple artifact to confirm completion of all defined steps in a recurring task. What should be used?

correct.text: Checklist
correct.why: A checklist verifies that each required step was completed.

correct.text: A checklist
correct.why: It confirms completion of defined steps in repeatable work.

correct.text: Use a task checklist.
correct.why: Checklists help ensure recurring activities follow every required step.

distractors.text: Flowchart
distractors.trap: Shows sequence, not completed steps.

distractors.text: Histogram
distractors.trap: Displays frequency distributions, not task completion.

distractors.text: Facilitation
distractors.trap: Helps groups decide, not verify steps.

distractors.text: Scatter diagram
distractors.trap: Shows variable relationships, not checklist completion.

distractors.text: Affinity diagram
distractors.trap: Groups ideas; it does not confirm execution.

distractors.text: Control chart
distractors.trap: Monitors process stability, not step completion.

explanation: A checklist is used to validate that each required step of a recurring task has been completed, unlike a flowchart, histogram, or facilitation.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: You learn that a functional manager is assigning one of your team members to a two-week task on another project. Losing this resource for two weeks would jeopardize your schedule. How should you respond?
A department manager plans to pull a project team member onto another initiative for two weeks. The absence would put the current schedule at risk. What should the project manager do first?
During execution, a shared resource is about to be reassigned by their functional manager for two weeks. The project manager expects a schedule impact. What is the best response?
A key contributor may be unavailable for two weeks because their line manager wants them on another project. How should the project manager handle the resource conflict?

correct.text: Review the resource management plan before negotiating with the functional manager.
correct.why: The plan defines resource allocation and negotiation guidance.

correct.text: Use the resource management plan to guide the negotiation.
correct.why: Resource conflicts should be handled according to the agreed plan.

correct.text: Consult the resource plan and negotiate with the reporting manager.
correct.why: The plan guides how shared resources are obtained and managed.

distractors.text: Submit a change request for a replacement resource.
distractors.trap: Negotiation should be attempted before formal replacement.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to rank the two projects.
distractors.trap: Escalates before using the resource plan.

distractors.text: Assign another team member to cover the work.
distractors.trap: Shifts work without resolving the conflict.

distractors.text: Update the schedule baseline for the delay.
distractors.trap: Accepts impact before attempting resolution.

distractors.text: Tell the team member to refuse the assignment.
distractors.trap: Bypasses the functional manager relationship.

distractors.text: Record the absence as an accepted risk.
distractors.trap: Treats a current resource issue passively.

explanation: The resource management plan defines how resources are categorized, allocated, and released, guiding the project manager on negotiating with the functional manager before escalating.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: During planning, the stakeholders failed to document an essential system feature. The client notices the omission during an early review of the deliverables. How should the project manager respond?
A needed product capability was not captured in the planning documents. During an early deliverable review, the customer identifies the missing feature. What should the project manager do?
On a software project, an important feature was left out of the documented scope. The client discovers the gap during an initial review. Which response is best?
Stakeholders overlooked a required feature during planning, and the omission is found after work has begun. How should the project manager handle it?

correct.text: Assess the impact and submit a change request.
correct.why: A missing required feature must go through formal change control.

correct.text: Analyze the impact and take it to change control.
correct.why: Scope additions require impact analysis and formal approval.

correct.text: Evaluate the feature's impact and raise a change request.
correct.why: The change process determines whether and how scope is updated.

distractors.text: Check the procurement plan for adding the feature.
distractors.trap: Procurement does not control missed scope.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to offer a price discount.
distractors.trap: A discount does not address the feature.

distractors.text: Negotiate a new price directly with the client.
distractors.trap: Repricing bypasses change control.

distractors.text: Add the feature immediately to satisfy the client.
distractors.trap: Implementation before approval is uncontrolled change.

distractors.text: Reject the feature because planning is complete.
distractors.trap: Required changes still need assessment.

distractors.text: Defer the feature to a future project automatically.
distractors.trap: Deferral requires analysis and approval.

explanation: The feature is required, so the project manager assesses its impact and routes it through the change control board, rather than offering discounts or repricing.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: A project team skipped user acceptance testing on a component because the product had worked in other projects. After implementation, the product is not operational. What should the project manager do to identify the source of the problem?
A team did not perform user acceptance testing because a similar solution had succeeded elsewhere. After rollout, the component fails. Which technique should identify the underlying reason?
On an internal systems project, the team reused a product without UAT and the implemented product does not work. What should the project manager use to find the source?
A component was assumed to be reliable based on previous projects, so acceptance testing was skipped. It fails after implementation. What should be done to determine why?

correct.text: Conduct a root cause analysis.
correct.why: Root cause analysis identifies the underlying reason for a defect.

correct.text: Perform root cause analysis.
correct.why: It traces the failure back to its true source.

correct.text: Use root cause analysis.
correct.why: This technique investigates why the problem actually occurred.

distractors.text: Review the quality metrics document.
distractors.trap: Metrics define measures, not failure causes.

distractors.text: Create a scatter diagram.
distractors.trap: Shows correlation between variables.

distractors.text: Perform a quality audit.
distractors.trap: Audits check process compliance.

distractors.text: Update the lessons learned register.
distractors.trap: Useful later, after cause is known.

distractors.text: Prepare a control chart.
distractors.trap: Assesses stability, not root source.

distractors.text: Revise the acceptance criteria.
distractors.trap: Does not explain the current failure.

explanation: Root cause analysis is the analytical technique used to determine the underlying reason for a defect, unlike quality metrics, a scatter diagram, or an audit.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: A manufacturing company receives complaints that a product is defective, and the sponsor suggests the complaints may be outliers. What should the project manager use to investigate the cause and quantity of defects?
A plant receives defect complaints, but the sponsor thinks they may be isolated cases. Which quality tool helps show how many defects occur by category or cause?
On a production project, customer complaints mention several defect types. The project manager needs to understand defect quantities and likely causes. What should be used?
A sponsor wants evidence about whether product defects are concentrated in specific causes rather than random outliers. Which tool is most appropriate?

correct.text: Histogram
correct.why: A histogram displays defect counts by category or cause.

correct.text: A histogram
correct.why: It shows quantities and helps rank defect categories.

correct.text: Use a histogram.
correct.why: Histograms reveal how often each defect cause occurs.

distractors.text: Flowchart
distractors.trap: Maps process steps, not defect quantities.

distractors.text: Affinity diagram
distractors.trap: Groups ideas without counting defects.

distractors.text: Control chart
distractors.trap: Shows process stability over time.

distractors.text: Scatter diagram
distractors.trap: Shows relationships between two variables.

distractors.text: Checklist
distractors.trap: Verifies steps; it does not rank defects.

distractors.text: Cause-and-effect diagram
distractors.trap: Explores causes but does not quantify them.

explanation: A histogram displays the number of defects per deliverable and ranks their causes, quantifying the problem, unlike a flowchart, affinity diagram, or control chart.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: A project was cancelled during its initiation phase because adequate resources were not available. How should the project manager respond?
During initiation, an organization cancels a proposed project after determining that needed resources are unavailable. What should the project manager do next?
A newly initiated project will not continue because the organization cannot staff it. Which response should guide the project manager?
A project is stopped before planning begins due to lack of resources. How should the project manager handle the cancellation?

correct.text: Refer to the company's project closure guidelines.
correct.why: Organizational closure procedures guide proper project termination.

correct.text: Follow the organization's closure guidelines.
correct.why: Cancelled projects should be closed using approved procedures.

correct.text: Use company guidance for closing the project.
correct.why: Closure guidance defines required steps even for cancelled work.

distractors.text: Notify all assigned team members immediately.
distractors.trap: No team was assigned during initiation.

distractors.text: Perform a new risk analysis on termination.
distractors.trap: Cancellation has already been decided.

distractors.text: Reassign resources and reallocate the budget.
distractors.trap: Resources and budget were not allocated.

distractors.text: Create a detailed project management plan.
distractors.trap: Planning is unnecessary after cancellation.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to restart the project later.
distractors.trap: Does not address required closure.

distractors.text: Hold a kickoff meeting to announce cancellation.
distractors.trap: Kickoff is inappropriate for a cancelled project.

explanation: Because the project was cancelled in initiation, the project manager follows the organization's project closure guidelines; the other options assume resources and assignments that never existed.

eco_task: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
topic: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
prompts: Midway through a three-year project, the oversight committee grows uncertain whether the final outcome will meet expectations and deliver the anticipated value. As the project manager, what proactive step can you take to address their concerns?
