A completed project handed over a new business service poorly, leaving the project manager to run it after closeout. What should the project manager have planned at initiation?

correct.text: Included a transition plan as a deliverable in project closure.
correct.why: A transition plan transfers operations to the responsible business team.

correct.text: Planned an operational transition deliverable for project closure.
correct.why: Closure should include handover of ongoing operations.

correct.text: Defined a transition plan deliverable for business handoff.
correct.why: Formal handoff prevents the PM from retaining operational responsibility.

distractors.text: Made the organizational structure revision a project deliverable.
distractors.trap: Structure changes do not ensure operational handoff.

distractors.text: Clarified the RACI chart with the sponsor at project start.
distractors.trap: Roles alone do not define transition activities.

distractors.text: Added the recruitment process to the project management plan.
distractors.trap: Hiring support is not the handover mechanism.

distractors.text: Asked operations to attend more project status meetings.
distractors.trap: Meeting attendance does not create transition ownership.

distractors.text: Extended the project until operations became comfortable.
distractors.trap: Extends responsibility instead of planning handoff.

distractors.text: Assigned the sponsor to run operations after completion.
distractors.trap: Sponsor ownership is not a closure transition plan.

explanation: Planning a transition plan as a closing deliverable ensures the new function is handed to the operations team, preventing the manager from being stuck running operations.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: On a predictive project now behind schedule and over budget, a key stakeholder has been submitting an unusually high number of change requests. What should the project manager do to help achieve the desired outcomes?
A waterfall project is slipping and exceeding its budget after one influential stakeholder submits many change requests. What should the project manager address?
A predictive initiative is struggling with cost and schedule performance, and excessive change requests from a major stakeholder appear to be a key driver. What should the project manager do?
A plan-driven project has deteriorating performance because many requested changes keep entering the process. How should the project manager improve the chance of meeting outcomes?

correct.text: Examine the change control process and implement improvements.
correct.why: Improved change control directly addresses excessive requests affecting performance.

correct.text: Review and strengthen the project's change control process.
correct.why: A stronger process helps manage scope changes causing overruns.

correct.text: Assess the change control approach and improve how changes are managed.
correct.why: The issue centers on uncontrolled change flow.

distractors.text: Review the risk management plan and create a risk-reduction plan.
distractors.trap: Risk planning does not control change volume.

distractors.text: Use contingency reserve to fund a fast-tracked activity.
distractors.trap: Reserves do not solve change control problems.

distractors.text: Escalate the stakeholder issue to the executive sponsor immediately.
distractors.trap: Escalation should follow direct process action.

distractors.text: Reject all future change requests from the stakeholder.
distractors.trap: Ignores valid changes and bypasses governance.

distractors.text: Add more team members to absorb the requested changes.
distractors.trap: More capacity does not fix uncontrolled changes.

distractors.text: Revise the baseline after every submitted change request.
distractors.trap: Baselines change only after approved changes.

explanation: The core issue is excessive change requests, so improving the change control process to better manage and streamline them addresses the root cause of the overruns.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Scope
topic: Plan and Manage Scope
prompts: A team spots a chance to enhance software with no budget or schedule impact and implements it without proper documentation, which later causes production issues. Which process should have been performed to prevent this?

Developers identify a small software improvement that appears to have no cost or schedule effect, so they implement it informally. The change later creates production problems. What process was missing?
A project team makes an undocumented enhancement because it seems beneficial and harmless to the plan. Later, the enhancement causes defects in production. Which process should have controlled it?
A team implements an unapproved software improvement after assuming it will not affect budget or schedule. Subsequent production issues show the impact was not assessed. What should have been done?

correct.text: Perform Integrated Change Control before implementing the enhancement
correct.why: It evaluates, approves, and documents changes before implementation.

correct.text: Use the Perform Integrated Change Control process for the change
correct.why: Formal change control ensures impacts are assessed and recorded.

correct.text: Route the enhancement through integrated change control first
correct.why: All changes need review and documentation before execution.

distractors.text: Control the schedule to confirm the enhancement has no timing impact
distractors.trap: Manages schedule performance, not change approval.

distractors.text: Perform qualitative risk analysis on the enhancement after release
distractors.trap: Prioritizes risks, not change approvals.

distractors.text: Plan scope management to define how future scope will be handled
distractors.trap: Defines process, not individual change control.

distractors.text: Validate scope to obtain formal acceptance of the software deliverable
distractors.trap: Focuses on deliverable acceptance.

distractors.text: Control quality to inspect the production defects after deployment
distractors.trap: Finds defects but does not approve changes.

distractors.text: Manage communications to notify stakeholders about the enhancement
distractors.trap: Distributes information, not change authorization.

explanation: Even zero-impact changes must go through Perform Integrated Change Control so they are reviewed, approved, and documented, preventing untracked changes from causing issues.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Procurement
topic: Plan and Manage Procurement
prompts: To reduce procurement time during a bid, a project manager awarded additional work to a current contractor. Other bidders complained that the process lacked transparency and fairness. What should the project manager have done to prevent this?

During a competitive bid for facility work, Lena gave extra scope to the incumbent supplier to save time. Several vendors later objected that they were not treated fairly. What should the project manager have done?

A project manager in a public-sector technology project bypassed the normal bid steps and extended work to an existing contractor. Competing bidders challenged the decision as unfair. What action would have prevented the issue?

While trying to accelerate a procurement, a project manager selected the current vendor for additional work before completing the planned bidding process. Other bidders questioned the transparency of the award. What should have been done?

correct.text: Follow the company's bidding procedure
correct.why: Established bidding procedures protect fairness, transparency, and equal opportunity for qualified sellers.

correct.text: Use the approved procurement bidding process
correct.why: The approved process keeps vendor selection transparent and defensible for all bidders.

correct.text: Apply the organization's formal bid procedure
correct.why: Formal procurement rules prevent favoritism and support an auditable vendor selection.

distractors.text: Invite all potential bidders to a clarification meeting
distractors.trap: A meeting alone does not replace the required bid process.

distractors.text: Award the work without negotiation to avoid delays
distractors.trap: Speed does not justify bypassing fair procurement controls.

distractors.text: Share the project objectives with every bidder
distractors.trap: Objectives help communication but do not ensure compliant selection.

distractors.text: Ask the incumbent contractor to justify the price
distractors.trap: Price justification does not address equal opportunity for bidders.

distractors.text: Escalate the bidder complaints after contract award
distractors.trap: Escalation after the fact does not prevent the fairness issue.

distractors.text: Document the time savings in the procurement file
distractors.trap: Documentation cannot make a nontransparent award fair.

explanation: Following the company's established bidding procedure ensures a fair, consistent, and transparent process that gives all bidders equal opportunity, avoiding the complaints.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: A pilot project is ready to close, and the team wants to capture the challenges they overcame and opportunities for future work. Where should they document this information?
As a small healthcare pilot moves into closure, the team wants to record obstacles, successes, and ideas that could help later efforts. Where should this be captured?
A trial implementation is ending, and the delivery team wants future projects to benefit from what they learned while resolving issues. What should they update?
Near the close of a pilot, the project manager asks where to record the team's challenges, workarounds, and future improvement opportunities. Which artifact is appropriate?

correct.text: Capture the information in the lessons learned log
correct.why: It captures project knowledge so future teams can reuse insights and avoid repeated problems.

correct.text: Update the lessons learned log with closure insights
correct.why: Lessons learned record what worked, what did not, and opportunities for future use.

correct.text: Record the experiences in the lessons learned register
correct.why: The register stores experience gained during the project for organizational reuse.

distractors.text: Update organizational process assets directly as the primary record
distractors.trap: Those may be updated later from lessons learned.

distractors.text: Add the information to a work performance report
distractors.trap: It communicates performance, not reusable learning.

distractors.text: Revise the project management plan with the team's closure experiences
distractors.trap: It guides execution, not closure learning.

distractors.text: Keep the challenges and workarounds in the issue log
distractors.trap: It tracks active issues, not broader learning.

distractors.text: Record the improvement opportunities in the risk register
distractors.trap: It tracks risks, not completed-project insights.

distractors.text: Summarize the learning in the final procurement report
distractors.trap: Procurement reporting is too narrow.

explanation: Challenges overcome and future opportunities are recorded in the lessons learned log so the knowledge can be shared with and reused by other project teams.

eco_task: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
topic: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
prompts: At the start of a project, a stakeholder is concerned the initiative may not deliver value to the organization. What should the project manager do first to increase the chances of success?
During initiation, an operations stakeholder tells Miguel the project may not produce meaningful organizational value. What should the project manager do first?
A sponsor's delegate questions whether a new internal platform will be worth the investment before execution begins. What is the project manager's best first response?
Early in a project, a stakeholder doubts that the expected outcomes justify the effort. Which action should the project manager take first?

correct.text: Review the business case and benefits management plan with the stakeholder
correct.why: These artifacts directly explain the project's purpose, expected value, and benefit path.

correct.text: Discuss the business case and benefits plan with the stakeholder
correct.why: The discussion addresses value concerns using the approved rationale and benefits approach.

correct.text: Walk through the business case and planned benefits
correct.why: Reviewing planned benefits first aligns the stakeholder to why the project exists.

distractors.text: Provide frequent project status communications to stakeholders
distractors.trap: Status updates do not answer the initial value concern.

distractors.text: Schedule product training for all impacted users
distractors.trap: Training is premature before addressing project value.

distractors.text: Perform an audit to confirm benefits realization
distractors.trap: Audits occur later, after benefits can be measured.

distractors.text: Add more success metrics to the project dashboard
distractors.trap: Metrics display progress but do not validate the business rationale.

distractors.text: Escalate the concern to the steering committee
distractors.trap: Escalation is premature before reviewing the value documents.

distractors.text: Ask the team to accelerate the first deliverable
distractors.trap: Faster delivery does not prove organizational value.

explanation: Reviewing the business case and benefits management plan with the stakeholder demonstrates the project's clear purpose and planned benefits, directly addressing their value concern early.

eco_task: Manage Project Issues
topic: Manage Project Issues
prompts: You built an agile plan to deliver a product quickly, but the project is complex and the requirements are unclear, putting the schedule at risk. What should you do to get the project back on track?
An agile team is trying to release a complex analytics product quickly, but unclear requirements are causing schedule pressure. What should the project manager do next?
Priya's team planned rapid agile delivery, yet the product requirements remain ambiguous and the release date is now at risk. What action best addresses the problem?
A complex agile initiative is falling behind because the team is not aligned on what the requirements mean. What should be done to recover the effort?

correct.text: Review the product backlog with the product owner and team
correct.why: Joint backlog review clarifies requirements and priorities at the source of the schedule risk.

correct.text: Refine the backlog with the product owner and delivery team
correct.why: Backlog refinement resolves ambiguity before the team continues building uncertain work.

correct.text: Work with the product owner and team to review backlog priorities
correct.why: Shared review clarifies what matters most and reduces requirement-driven delay.

distractors.text: Rebaseline the schedule to absorb the expected delays
distractors.trap: It treats timing symptoms without clarifying requirements.

distractors.text: Increase standup frequency and require daily status updates
distractors.trap: More reporting does not clarify ambiguous backlog items.

distractors.text: Add more iterative and incremental features to the plan
distractors.trap: More work can worsen ambiguity if priorities remain unclear.

distractors.text: Assign a senior developer to decide the missing details
distractors.trap: Technical judgment cannot replace product ownership and team alignment.

distractors.text: Escalate the schedule risk to the sponsor immediately
distractors.trap: Escalation is premature before addressing the backlog root cause.

distractors.text: Freeze requirements until the next release cycle begins
distractors.trap: Freezing unclear requirements locks in confusion.

explanation: The root cause is unclear requirements causing a mis-prioritized backlog. Reviewing the backlog with the product owner and team realigns and splits work to resolve the status.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Procurement
topic: Plan and Manage Procurement
prompts: During repair work on a completed facility, the root cause of the damage is traced to failures made during the original construction phase. The same contractor is performing the repairs, and the CEO wants to negotiate a reduced price. How should the project manager proceed?

A completed warehouse needs repairs, and analysis shows the damage came from mistakes in the original build. The original contractor is doing the repair work, and leadership wants a lower price. What should the project manager do?

During post-completion repairs on a plant, the team confirms that the defect resulted from the contractor's earlier construction work. The same contractor will perform the fix. How should pricing be handled?

A facility repair is traced back to failures in the original construction phase. Since the same supplier is handling the repair, the executive team asks for a fair reduced price. What is the best approach?

correct.text: Negotiate repair work using a shared risk-reward arrangement
correct.why: The contractor's role in the defect supports a collaborative agreement that fairly shares cost.

correct.text: Negotiate a shared risk-reward deal with the contractor
correct.why: Shared risk-reward balances accountability for the defect with efficient completion of repairs.

correct.text: Use shared risk-reward terms for the repair negotiation
correct.why: This structure reflects contractor responsibility while keeping both parties aligned on the repair.

distractors.text: Cancel the repair contract and compensate prior construction work
distractors.trap: Cancellation does not fairly resolve the new repair scope.

distractors.text: Continue without raising the prior defect issue
distractors.trap: Ignoring root cause forfeits legitimate negotiation leverage.

distractors.text: Deduct prior supervision costs from the repair price
distractors.trap: Arbitrary deductions are not a defensible contract basis.

distractors.text: Reopen the original project and charge all costs there
distractors.trap: The original project is completed and cannot absorb new scope automatically.

distractors.text: Terminate the contractor for default before repair work starts
distractors.trap: Termination may delay repair and is disproportionate without due process.

distractors.text: Request competitive bids for the full repair package
distractors.trap: The scenario asks how to negotiate with the same contractor.

explanation: Because the same contractor caused the construction failures, a collaborative shared risk-reward negotiation ensures the repairs are completed efficiently at a cost that is fair to both parties.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: An innovative project uses AI to identify chemical compounds for new products. The AI processing run takes anywhere from three days to two weeks with unpredictable results. The goal is to deliver at least one product specification within a fixed two-month window and a budget that cannot be increased. What should the project manager do to maximize business value within that fixed budget?

correct.text: Plan an iteration right after AI processing to analyze and prioritize the development goals.
correct.why: Adapting after results arrive lets the team focus the remaining fixed budget on the highest-value specifications.

distractors.text: Cap the specification iterations at 51.5% of the overall project duration.
distractors.trap: An arbitrary time cap ignores the unpredictable AI runtime and does not target value.

distractors.text: Cap the AI processing at 34.0% of the overall project budget.
distractors.trap: Constraining the core discovery step could produce sub-optimal, low-value results.

distractors.text: Plan the specification work as a fixed-duration predictive task to guarantee delivery.
distractors.trap: Predictive planning ignores the inherent unpredictability of the AI discovery.

explanation: The AI results are unpredictable, so planning an adaptive iteration immediately after processing lets the team analyze findings and direct the fixed budget toward the most valuable work.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project's budget at completion (BAC) is US$103,310, but the estimate at completion (EAC) has risen to US$123,310. If the schedule performance index (SPI) equals 1, what is the cost performance index (CPI)?

correct.text: Less than 1
correct.why: EAC exceeding BAC means the project is over budget, which corresponds to a CPI below 1.

distractors.text: Equal to 1.2
distractors.trap: Confuses the EAC/BAC ratio direction; a higher EAC does not mean CPI above 1.

distractors.text: Equal to 1
distractors.trap: A CPI of 1 would mean on-budget, contradicting the higher EAC.

distractors.text: Greater than 1
distractors.trap: A CPI above 1 signals under budget, the opposite of the rising EAC.

explanation: When EAC is greater than BAC the project is forecast to overrun its budget, which happens when the CPI is less than 1.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: After a large equipment installation project has been transitioned and completed under budget with unused contingency reserves, the customer asks the project manager to add operational team-building costs to the project budget to keep the operations team performing well. How should the project manager respond?

A completed system installation has already been handed over and has unused contingency funds. The customer asks to use the remaining budget for operations team morale activities. What should the project manager do?

After transition to operations, a project finishes below budget with contingency reserves remaining. A department manager asks to charge post-project team-building expenses to the project. How should the project manager respond?
An equipment rollout is complete and financially favorable, but the client wants leftover contingency used for ongoing operations support events. What is the appropriate response?

correct.text: Decline the request and close the project financials
correct.why: Completed project funds cannot be used for operational activities outside the approved scope.

correct.text: Reject the expenditure and finalize project finances
correct.why: Unused contingency is not available for post-closure operational team costs.

correct.text: Do not fund it and close out the financial accounts
correct.why: The project is complete, and the request is operational, not project scope.

distractors.text: Submit the request through integrated change control
distractors.trap: Change control is not appropriate after transition and completion.

distractors.text: Approve the expense to maintain customer satisfaction
distractors.trap: Customer satisfaction cannot justify out-of-scope spending.

distractors.text: Record the request in the lessons learned repository
distractors.trap: A lesson entry does not resolve the funding decision.

distractors.text: Transfer the contingency reserve to the operations manager
distractors.trap: Contingency reserves are not a discretionary operations fund.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to add the cost to the baseline
distractors.trap: The project is already complete and transitioned.

distractors.text: Use the savings because the project is under budget
distractors.trap: Being under budget does not expand approved scope.

explanation: Contingency reserves are reserved for project risks, not operational items, so after completion the request should be declined and the remaining budget released to the organization.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Scope
topic: Plan and Manage Scope
prompts: While testing a component, a senior tester identifies added functionality that would safeguard the future performance of the project's solution, creating a scope change. What type of change request should be raised to incorporate it?
During validation, a quality analyst recommends extra functionality that would protect the product's future performance. The change would add scope. What type of change request is this?
A test lead finds an enhancement that could reduce the chance of later performance problems, although no current defect exists. Which change request category applies?
In component testing, a team member proposes additional capability to avoid future performance degradation. If approved, it changes scope. What kind of request should be submitted?

correct.text: Preventive action
correct.why: It proactively reduces the chance of future performance problems before they occur.

correct.text: A preventive action request
correct.why: The proposed change protects future performance rather than correcting current variance.

correct.text: Submit it as preventive action
correct.why: Preventive action addresses potential future issues, not existing defects.

distractors.text: Corrective action
distractors.trap: Corrective action realigns current performance with the plan.

distractors.text: Defect repair
distractors.trap: No nonconforming deliverable has been identified.

distractors.text: Scope update
distractors.trap: That is an outcome, not the change request type.

distractors.text: Configuration audit
distractors.trap: An audit verifies items; it does not classify the request.

distractors.text: Requirements traceability update
distractors.trap: Traceability documents impact but is not the change category.

distractors.text: Quality control measurement
distractors.trap: Measurements record results, not proposed future protection.

explanation: A change intended to prevent a potential future performance problem is classified as a preventive action, distinct from corrective action or defect repair.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Procurement
topic: Plan and Manage Procurement
prompts: A building's construction is well defined by detailed plans and written specifications. The work will be delivered over two years in a high-inflation environment. Which contract type should the project manager use?
A municipal office build has complete drawings and specifications, but delivery will span two years while inflation is high. Which contract type best fits?
Nora is procuring a clearly specified construction project with a long delivery period in an inflationary market. What contract type should she recommend?
The scope for a facility build is stable and fully documented, but costs may shift significantly during the two-year execution period. Which contract is most appropriate?

correct.text: Fixed price with economic price adjustment (FPEPA) contract
correct.why: FPEPA keeps defined-scope pricing fixed while allowing agreed inflation adjustments over time.

correct.text: FPEPA contract
correct.why: It suits well-defined work with a long period exposed to economic changes.

correct.text: Fixed-price contract with economic price adjustment
correct.why: Predefined adjustment terms address inflation risk without making scope cost-reimbursable.

distractors.text: Cost plus incentive fee (CPIF) contract
distractors.trap: Cost-reimbursable contracts fit uncertain scope, not well-defined work.

distractors.text: Firm fixed price (FFP) contract
distractors.trap: FFP leaves inflation risk with no economic adjustment.

distractors.text: Time and materials (T&M) contract
distractors.trap: T&M fits undefined or small efforts, not specified construction.

distractors.text: Cost plus fixed fee (CPFF) contract
distractors.trap: It shifts cost risk to the buyer unnecessarily.

distractors.text: Fixed price incentive fee (FPIF) contract
distractors.trap: Incentives do not directly handle broad inflation risk.

distractors.text: Indefinite delivery indefinite quantity contract
distractors.trap: The quantity and scope are already well defined.

explanation: With well-defined scope but multi-year delivery in a high-inflation environment, FPEPA keeps a fixed price while permitting predefined adjustments for inflation.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: While reviewing project documentation, a project manager discovers that modifications were made without proper approvals. What is the most appropriate next step?
A project manager audits documentation and finds several changes were implemented before approval was obtained. What should the project manager review first?
During a records check, Marco notices that approved baselines do not match modifications already made by the team. What is the best next step?
A documentation review reveals that some project changes bypassed the normal approval path. Which action should the project manager take next?

correct.text: Review the change management plan
correct.why: It defines how changes are evaluated, approved, documented, and handled.

correct.text: Consult the change management plan
correct.why: The plan provides the governing process for responding to unapproved changes.

correct.text: Use the change management plan to guide the response
correct.why: Unapproved modifications should be addressed according to the defined change process.

distractors.text: Escalate the issue to the portfolio management office
distractors.trap: Escalation is premature before checking the governing process.

distractors.text: Review the requirements management plan
distractors.trap: It governs requirements, not unauthorized change handling.

distractors.text: Escalate the issue directly to the executive sponsor
distractors.trap: This skips the project change control process.

distractors.text: Update the issue log and continue execution
distractors.trap: Logging alone does not determine the required change response.

distractors.text: Ask the team to reverse all modifications immediately
distractors.trap: Reversal may be wrong before impact and process review.

distractors.text: Revise the project baseline to match the work
distractors.trap: Baselines cannot be changed simply to absorb unauthorized work.

explanation: Unapproved changes are a process breakdown, so the PM first reviews the change management plan to determine how such changes should have been, and now should be, handled.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: The first iteration of an agile project has produced an initial release increment that is now complete, and the team is eager to hand it to the customer. How should the product be confirmed as ready for customer use?
After the first iteration, an agile team has an initial increment and wants to release it to a client. How should readiness for customer use be confirmed?
A software squad finishes its first releasable increment and is preparing to hand over the MVP. What confirms that it is ready?
The first sprint has produced a complete initial release increment, and stakeholders want it shipped. Which readiness check should the team rely on?

correct.text: The team agrees the MVP meets the definition of done
correct.why: The whole agile team confirms the increment satisfies agreed completion criteria before release.

correct.text: The project team confirms it satisfies the DoD
correct.why: Definition of done is the shared gate for release-ready increments.

correct.text: The team validates the increment against the definition of done
correct.why: Readiness comes from meeting the team's agreed DoD, not a single person's opinion.

distractors.text: The agile coach determines that all requirements are satisfied
distractors.trap: The coach facilitates but does not decide release readiness alone.

distractors.text: The senior manager and customer authorize the product
distractors.trap: Authorization is not the team readiness criterion.

distractors.text: The product owner decides it passed all quality tests
distractors.trap: Testing alone is only part of done.

distractors.text: The sponsor confirms the release budget is available
distractors.trap: Funding does not prove the increment is releasable.

distractors.text: The scrum master declares the sprint closed
distractors.trap: Closing a sprint does not certify product readiness.

distractors.text: The customer support lead accepts the handover package
distractors.trap: Handover acceptance is not the agile readiness standard.

explanation: The definition of done is the agreed checklist for release readiness, and in an agile context the whole team collectively confirms the increment meets it.

eco_task: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
topic: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
prompts: A project is nearing completion, and a stakeholder wants to understand how the project's outcomes will affect the organization. Which document should the project manager review with the stakeholder?
As a project approaches closure, an executive asks how the delivered outcomes will translate into organizational impact. Which document should the project manager use?
Near completion of a transformation initiative, a business stakeholder wants to review when benefits will occur and how they affect the company. What should be reviewed?
A stakeholder asks, late in the project, how the final results are expected to produce value for the organization. Which artifact best answers this?

correct.text: Review the benefits management plan with the stakeholder
correct.why: It defines expected benefits, timing, ownership, and impact on the organization.

correct.text: Use the benefits management plan to discuss organizational impact
correct.why: The plan explains how outcomes are converted into measurable organizational benefits.

correct.text: Use the benefits management plan for benefit realization details
correct.why: It connects project outcomes to business value and benefit realization.

distractors.text: Review the business case that originally justified the project
distractors.trap: It justified starting, not realization details.

distractors.text: Use the project charter to explain expected outcome impact
distractors.trap: It authorizes work but lacks realization detail.

distractors.text: Review the assumption log for constraints affecting the results
distractors.trap: Tracks assumptions, not benefits impact.

distractors.text: Use the scope baseline to describe organizational outcome effects
distractors.trap: Defines approved scope, not business benefits.

distractors.text: Review the stakeholder engagement plan with the stakeholder
distractors.trap: Manages engagement, not benefit delivery.

distractors.text: Use the project schedule to show when business benefits occur
distractors.trap: Shows work timing, not benefit impact.

explanation: The benefits management plan is the document that describes how and when project benefits will be realized and their impact on the organization, making it the right one to review.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: A design project is ready to be transferred to production. What activity is required for phase closure?
A product design phase is complete and the work is moving into production. Which activity should be completed as part of closing the phase?
Before a design initiative transitions to manufacturing, the project manager checks closure requirements. What must be documented?
A design deliverable has met acceptance criteria and is ready for production handoff. Which closure activity is required?

correct.text: Record the impacts of tailoring on any process
correct.why: Closure includes documenting how tailored processes affected project execution and transition.

correct.text: Document process tailoring impacts
correct.why: Tailoring decisions and their impacts should be recorded during phase or project closure.

correct.text: Capture tailoring impacts in the closure documentation
correct.why: Final closure records should reflect how tailored processes affected the work.

distractors.text: Determine the summary budget
distractors.trap: Budget summarization does not address phase transition closure needs.

distractors.text: Manage quality
distractors.trap: Quality should be managed throughout, not newly started at closure.

distractors.text: Ensure preventive actions are completed
distractors.trap: Open preventive actions should be resolved before closure readiness.

distractors.text: Create the initial project charter
distractors.trap: The charter belongs at initiation, not phase closure.

distractors.text: Develop the stakeholder engagement strategy
distractors.trap: Engagement planning is not the required closure record.

distractors.text: Approve a new production baseline
distractors.trap: Baseline approval is not the closure documentation activity.

explanation: As part of integration management at project closure, the PM prepares the final report recording the impacts of transitioning and tailoring, since quality and preventive actions are already resolved beforehand.

eco_task: Integrate Project Planning Activities
topic: Integrate Project Planning Activities
prompts: You are managing several interconnected projects where some depend on specific outputs from others. To avoid bottlenecks and support success, you must review the consolidated plans for dependencies. What is the most appropriate action?
A program coordinator is reviewing several related projects, and some teams need deliverables from other teams before they can proceed. What should be done to support planning?
Multiple projects in a portfolio are linked through handoffs, and delays in one could block another. Which action best helps avoid bottlenecks?
You are consolidating plans for interdependent initiatives and need to make cross-project handoffs visible. What is the most appropriate step?

correct.text: Identify and document all inter-project dependencies
correct.why: Documented dependencies make cross-project handoffs visible for planning, risk review, and coordination.

correct.text: Record the dependencies among the projects
correct.why: Capturing dependencies clarifies which outputs enable other project work.

correct.text: Document the inter-project output dependencies
correct.why: Clear dependency records help teams plan around bottlenecks and shared risks.

distractors.text: Adjust project timelines to remove the dependencies
distractors.trap: Timeline shifts rarely eliminate real output dependencies.

distractors.text: Eliminate all potential inter-project dependencies
distractors.trap: Removing all dependencies is often impractical or harmful.

distractors.text: Ensure all projects can proceed independently
distractors.trap: Full independence may be impossible in interconnected work.

distractors.text: Assign one team to own every shared deliverable
distractors.trap: Ownership changes do not document or manage all dependencies.

distractors.text: Escalate every dependency to the steering committee
distractors.trap: Escalating all dependencies is inefficient before analysis.

distractors.text: Delay dependent projects until all outputs are complete
distractors.trap: Blanket delays create waste and do not improve planning.

explanation: The PM should identify and document all inter-project dependencies, which clarifies which outputs affect others, supports planning and communication, and ensures dependency risks are flagged.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Schedule
topic: Plan and Manage Schedule
prompts: A project is installing three MRI units in a hospital; one has passed acceptance testing and two are under construction. The client submits a change order for a fourth unit with slightly different requirements. How should the project manager most accurately estimate the additional unit's delivery?

A hospital equipment project has one scanner accepted and two still being built. The client requests another unit with some different requirements. Which estimating approach gives the most accurate delivery estimate?
During a medical installation project, the customer adds a fourth unit that is similar to earlier units but not identical. How should the project manager estimate the added work most accurately?
A project team has experience installing the first units, but a new change order adds another unit with unique requirements. What should the project manager do to estimate its delivery?

correct.text: Break the work into smaller tasks and estimate each individually
correct.why: Bottom-up estimating captures task-level detail for the new unit's specific requirements.

correct.text: Use bottom-up estimating for the added unit
correct.why: Estimating detailed work packages gives the most accurate view of the changed scope.

correct.text: Decompose the added work and estimate each component
correct.why: Task-level estimates account for differences between the new unit and earlier units.

distractors.text: Rely on historical data from similar past projects
distractors.trap: Analogous estimates may miss the new unit's differences.

distractors.text: Apply industry formulas based on project characteristics
distractors.trap: Parametric models can miss change-specific schedule impacts.

distractors.text: Compare the new unit to existing ones and adjust estimates
distractors.trap: Relative estimating may overlook important requirement differences.

distractors.text: Use the average duration of the first three units
distractors.trap: Averages ignore the fourth unit's different requirements.

distractors.text: Ask the client for the required completion date
distractors.trap: A desired date is not an estimating method.

distractors.text: Add a standard contingency percentage to the prior estimate
distractors.trap: Contingency does not create an accurate base estimate.

explanation: Decomposing the fourth unit into individual tasks (bottom-up estimating) yields the most accurate delivery estimate because it accounts for its specific, differing requirements.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project manager planning a strategic predictive project has suddenly fallen ill, and the baseline must reach the customer in three days. The PMO director must reassign the planning to either Project Manager A (highly experienced in Scrum only, available immediately for 2 days) or Project Manager B (moderately experienced in infrastructure upgrades, available tomorrow for a full week). Whom should the director choose, and why?

correct.text: B, who understands predictive planning complexity and can draw on a network of subject matter experts
correct.why: Predictive baseline estimation relies on expert input and a predictive mindset, which B provides.

distractors.text: A, who is available immediately and can plan quickly and correctly
distractors.trap: Availability alone does not qualify someone for predictive planning.

distractors.text: A, who is more current with the latest project planning trends and methodologies
distractors.trap: Agile currency does not equip A for predictive baseline work.

distractors.text: B, who could keep working longer if the submission deadline is missed
distractors.trap: Planning for a missed deadline is the wrong basis for selection.

explanation: Predictive planning demands expert-driven estimation and a predictive mindset; B's understanding and SME network make B the right choice, not mere availability or agile currency.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: Reviewing status reports, a project manager sees that over six months one developer's coding quality is statistically lower than another's on the same task. Which tool should the project manager use to trace the problem back to its root cause?
A software project manager notices a persistent quality gap between two developers performing the same type of work. Which tool helps analyze the underlying causes?
Status data over several months shows one engineer's output has more quality issues than a peer's on comparable tasks. What tool should be used to investigate root cause?
A delivery lead wants to understand why one team member's defect rate is consistently worse than another's for similar assignments. Which quality tool is appropriate?

correct.text: Use an Ishikawa diagram to organize possible causes
correct.why: A fishbone diagram organizes potential causes to trace a problem to root causes.

correct.text: Apply a cause-and-effect diagram to explore contributing factors
correct.why: It systematically explores contributing factors behind a quality problem.

correct.text: Use a fishbone diagram to investigate root-cause categories
correct.why: It groups possible causes so the team can identify the root cause.

distractors.text: Use a histogram to display the distribution of coding defects
distractors.trap: Shows defect distribution, not root causes.

distractors.text: Use a control chart to evaluate process stability over time
distractors.trap: Shows stability, not why defects occur.

distractors.text: Use a scatter diagram to test relationships between variables
distractors.trap: Shows relationships, not causal categories.

distractors.text: Use a Pareto chart to rank defect categories by frequency
distractors.trap: Prioritizes categories without tracing causes.

distractors.text: Use checksheets to collect defect counts during coding work
distractors.trap: Collects data but does not analyze causes.

distractors.text: Use a flowchart to map the development process steps
distractors.trap: Maps process steps, not root-cause categories.

explanation: An Ishikawa (fishbone) diagram identifies the root cause of a problem, whereas histograms, control charts, and scatter diagrams display symptoms or relationships.

eco_task: Integrate Project Planning Activities
topic: Integrate Project Planning Activities
prompts: A team in a highly regulated industry is frustrated that the compliance department keeps imposing documentation and process steps that disrupt their preferred way of working. The team asks the project manager to intervene. What approach should the project manager take?
A medical-device team complains that regulatory documentation is slowing their normal delivery flow. They want the project manager to push back on compliance. What should the project manager do?
In a regulated banking project, the delivery team feels compliance activities are interrupting the way they prefer to work. How should the project manager respond?
A product team is frustrated by required compliance reviews and extra process steps. They ask the project manager to reduce the burden. Which approach is best?

correct.text: Help the team see compliance as essential to success and value creation
correct.why: Positioning compliance as value-critical preserves regulatory adherence while improving team alignment.

correct.text: Frame compliance work as part of delivering project value
correct.why: Compliance is not separate from value when regulatory acceptance is required.

correct.text: Show how compliance supports the project's overall value
correct.why: Connecting required controls to outcomes encourages collaboration without weakening compliance.

distractors.text: Push back on compliance and cut required documents
distractors.trap: Reducing required controls creates noncompliance risk.

distractors.text: Run a daily workshop to finish compliance tasks quickly
distractors.trap: Daily compliance sessions may further disrupt delivery.

distractors.text: Invite compliance staff to every daily sync meeting
distractors.trap: Full daily attendance is inefficient and may not solve alignment.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to exempt the team from process steps
distractors.trap: Required compliance steps cannot be bypassed for convenience.

distractors.text: Delay compliance documentation until final approval
distractors.trap: Late compliance work increases rework and acceptance risk.

distractors.text: Let the team choose which controls add value
distractors.trap: Teams cannot selectively ignore regulatory controls.

explanation: Compliance is the compliance department's responsibility and integral to project value; the project manager should build the team's understanding and collaboration rather than cut documentation or over-schedule compliance work.

eco_task: Manage Project Issues
topic: Manage Project Issues
prompts: During testing, a team member discovers that some requirements were not met. Adding work to satisfy them now could cause a large time and cost overrun. Which action should come first?
A tester finds that several accepted requirements are missing from the solution, and fixing them may significantly affect budget and schedule. What should the project manager do first?
Late in validation, the team identifies unmet requirements. Addressing them now could cause major cost and time impacts. Which step comes before escalation or updates?
During user testing, unmet needs are discovered and the team expects substantial overruns if they are added now. What is the first appropriate action?

correct.text: Perform an impact analysis
correct.why: Impact analysis quantifies schedule, cost, scope, and risk effects before decisions are made.

correct.text: Analyze the impact of the unmet requirements
correct.why: The project manager needs impact information before escalation, meetings, or log updates.

correct.text: Assess the impact before deciding next steps
correct.why: Informed decisions require understanding the consequences of satisfying the requirements now.

distractors.text: Escalate the issue to top management
distractors.trap: Escalation before impact assessment is premature.

distractors.text: Call a stakeholder meeting
distractors.trap: Stakeholders need impact data before meaningful decisions.

distractors.text: Revise the risk log
distractors.trap: Risk updates should use the impact analysis results.

distractors.text: Ask the team to add the missing work immediately
distractors.trap: Starting work before analysis may worsen overruns.

distractors.text: Reject the requirements because they are late
distractors.trap: Rejection is a decision that requires impact understanding.

distractors.text: Update the schedule baseline to include the work
distractors.trap: Baseline changes require analysis and approval first.

explanation: The project manager should first assess the impact of the unmet requirements; that analysis then feeds escalation, stakeholder engagement, and risk log updates.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: A project manager is starting a financial software project in a highly competitive environment. Compliance regulations are clear, but the requirements are not, and the customer stresses they need results as soon as possible. Which implementation approach should the project manager choose?
A fintech customer needs usable results quickly. Regulatory rules are understood, but product requirements are still emerging. Which delivery approach should the project manager select?
A team is launching a financial platform where compliance constraints are known, market pressure is high, and customer needs are unclear. What implementation approach best fits?
At project start, a customer in a competitive market wants early results even though requirements are not fully defined. Compliance obligations are clear. Which approach should be used?

correct.text: Deliver the project in multiple increments
correct.why: Incremental delivery provides early usable value while requirements are refined through feedback.

correct.text: Use incremental delivery
correct.why: It releases value sooner and supports learning when requirements are unclear.

correct.text: Implement through a series of usable increments
correct.why: Usable increments meet urgency while allowing the team to refine needs over time.

distractors.text: Repeat and refine project phases across iterations
distractors.trap: Iteration alone may refine work without delivering usable value early.

distractors.text: Progress through a linear sequence of stages
distractors.trap: Linear delivery assumes requirements are stable upfront.

distractors.text: Embrace change and focus on delivering value
distractors.trap: This is a mindset, not an implementation approach.

distractors.text: Freeze requirements until compliance approval is complete
distractors.trap: Freezing unclear requirements delays value and learning.

distractors.text: Complete all analysis before starting development
distractors.trap: Upfront analysis conflicts with urgent need for results.

distractors.text: Use a single final release after full acceptance testing
distractors.trap: A single release delays value in a competitive environment.

explanation: Incremental delivery gives the customer usable functionality early, satisfying the urgency and letting unclear requirements be clarified through feedback, reducing rework.

eco_task: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
topic: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
prompts: An educational institution complains after ordering a software module from an agile company, arguing that billing for a partial delivery violated the Agile Manifesto because the customer was not involved in progress meetings. The customer's claim is supported by which of the four values of the Agile Manifesto?
A university customer says an agile vendor excluded them from progress discussions and still charged for a partial software delivery. Which Agile Manifesto value supports the customer's complaint?
After a partial module delivery, a school argues the supplier focused on billing terms while leaving the customer out of collaboration. Which agile value is most relevant?
A client disputes an agile software invoice because they were not included in progress meetings before partial delivery. Which of the four Agile Manifesto values does this invoke?

correct.text: Customer collaboration over contract negotiation
correct.why: The complaint centers on excluding the customer instead of collaborating throughout delivery.

correct.text: Customer collaboration rather than contract negotiation
correct.why: The issue contrasts customer involvement with reliance on billing and contract terms.

correct.text: Value customer collaboration over contract negotiation
correct.why: Agile values active customer engagement more than focusing on contractual disputes.

distractors.text: Processes maintaining a constant pace indefinitely
distractors.trap: Sustainable pace is a principle, not a Manifesto value.

distractors.text: Frequent software delivery in short periods
distractors.trap: Frequent delivery is a principle, not the cited value.

distractors.text: Working software as the primary measure of progress
distractors.trap: This principle is about measurement, not customer involvement.

distractors.text: Responding to change over following a plan
distractors.trap: The complaint is about collaboration, not changing plans.

distractors.text: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
distractors.trap: Relevant generally, but the stated issue is customer collaboration.

distractors.text: Working software over comprehensive documentation
distractors.trap: The dispute is not about documentation versus software.

explanation: The customer was not involved in progress meetings, so the relevant value is 'customer collaboration over contract negotiation'; the other options are agile principles, not the four values.

eco_task: Integrate Project Planning Activities
topic: Integrate Project Planning Activities
prompts: You are planning the next release for a delivery squad that has completed two releases, and the product backlog for the next release is confirmed. Which tool should you use to plan the duration of the next release?
A product team has completed two prior releases and now has a confirmed backlog for the next release. Which tool best supports estimating how long the release will take?
The backlog for an upcoming release is agreed, and the squad needs to plan release duration based on the work to be delivered. What should be used?
A delivery lead is preparing the next release after two completed releases. With the backlog confirmed, which planning tool helps estimate the release duration?

correct.text: A work breakdown structure that decomposes the backlog into estimable components
correct.why: Decomposition creates smaller work components that can be estimated and aggregated.

correct.text: A WBS to break the backlog into smaller work components
correct.why: Breaking work down supports more reliable duration estimates for the release.

correct.text: A work breakdown structure for estimating the backlog work
correct.why: The WBS organizes confirmed scope into pieces small enough to estimate.

distractors.text: A product roadmap showing high-level feature timing
distractors.trap: Roadmaps lack the detail needed for duration estimation.

distractors.text: A resource calendar showing team member availability
distractors.trap: Availability does not define or estimate the work.

distractors.text: A burndown chart tracking remaining work during delivery
distractors.trap: Burndowns track progress after work has started.

distractors.text: A stakeholder register listing release decision makers
distractors.trap: Stakeholder data does not estimate release work.

distractors.text: A risk matrix ranking release threats
distractors.trap: Risk ranking does not decompose work for estimating.

distractors.text: A communications plan for release reporting
distractors.trap: Reporting plans do not calculate release duration.

explanation: The WBS is a hierarchical decomposition of the work; breaking work into smaller components lets the PM estimate effort and duration, unlike a roadmap, resource calendar, or burndown chart.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Schedule
topic: Plan and Manage Schedule
prompts: The delivery team is determining dependencies among activities for an ongoing project. What should the team do first to manage these dependencies effectively?
On an active construction project, the team is mapping how activities depend on one another. What is the first step for managing those dependencies well?
A project team in a healthcare rollout is trying to understand activity relationships across the schedule. What should they do first?
During schedule planning for work already underway, several team leads begin identifying links between tasks. What is the best initial action?

correct.text: Assess and document the dependencies.
correct.why: Dependencies must be identified and recorded before tracking, communicating, or acting on them.

correct.text: Identify and record the dependencies.
correct.why: Managing dependencies starts with making them explicit in the project records.

correct.text: Evaluate and document the activity dependencies.
correct.why: The team needs a documented baseline before monitoring or communicating dependency impacts.

distractors.text: Monitor and track the dependencies.
distractors.trap: This happens after dependencies are known and documented.

distractors.text: Communicate the dependencies to the sponsor.
distractors.trap: Communication follows identification and documentation.

distractors.text: Assign project schedulers to every task.
distractors.trap: Adds staffing without addressing dependency identification.

distractors.text: Escalate all dependencies to the steering committee.
distractors.trap: Escalation is premature before assessment.

distractors.text: Approve schedule reserves for dependent activities.
distractors.trap: Reserve decisions require documented dependencies first.

distractors.text: Rebaseline the schedule around the dependencies.
distractors.trap: Rebaselining is not the first management step.

explanation: Assessing and documenting dependencies is the first step; the team then reviews and updates that list throughout the project. Monitoring and communicating are subsequent activities.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Schedule
topic: Plan and Manage Schedule
prompts: A vendor tells the project manager that an outsourced deliverable will arrive six months late. The vendor says it prioritized other deliverables with stated dependencies, while this one had none. What should the project manager do to prevent this in the future?

A supplier explains that a contracted component is six months behind because work with explicit dependencies was handled first. This deliverable had no dependencies listed. What should the project manager change going forward?

During a technology project, an external provider delays a deliverable by six months because no dependency information was attached to it. How should the project manager avoid similar vendor sequencing problems?
An outsourced work package is badly delayed after the vendor gives priority to items that had documented dependencies. What should the project manager do for future outsourced work?

correct.text: Specify any dependencies where they exist.
correct.why: Explicit dependencies help vendors sequence work according to project priorities.

correct.text: Document applicable dependencies for vendor deliverables.
correct.why: Vendors can only prioritize dependency-driven work when those links are clearly stated.

correct.text: Make each relevant dependency explicit to the vendor.
correct.why: Clear dependency information prevents vendors from treating related deliverables as standalone work.

distractors.text: Monitor dependencies and respond to issues.
distractors.trap: This reacts after the sequencing problem appears.

distractors.text: Require all new dependencies to be finish-to-start.
distractors.trap: The relationship type was not the issue.

distractors.text: Hold a meeting to inform the customer.
distractors.trap: This communicates delay but does not prevent recurrence.

distractors.text: Replace vendors that miss dependency-driven work.
distractors.trap: Punishes the vendor without fixing unclear inputs.

distractors.text: Add penalty clauses for every outsourced deliverable.
distractors.trap: Contract penalties do not clarify dependencies.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to rank all vendor deliverables.
distractors.trap: Priority ranking cannot replace dependency definition.

explanation: The deliverable slipped because no dependency was stated; specifying dependencies where they exist, and keeping that list updated throughout the project, prevents the vendor from deprioritizing the work.

eco_task: Integrate Project Planning Activities
topic: Integrate Project Planning Activities
prompts: You developed a schedule for a project spanning several departments, meeting each team member individually to build the WBS. After finalizing it, you find that some tasks are redundant. What should you have done to avoid this?

A cross-department project WBS was built through separate one-on-one discussions with specialists. Once complete, it contains duplicate activities. What planning approach would have helped prevent this?
For a multi-team banking project, you interviewed experts separately while creating the WBS. Later, overlapping tasks appear in the plan. What should have been done differently?
After building a WBS from individual sessions with department representatives, the project manager discovers redundant work packages. What would have reduced this problem?

correct.text: Hold joint sessions for specialists to review the WBS together.
correct.why: Joint review exposes overlap between teams and builds shared understanding.

correct.text: Facilitate a shared WBS review with all specialists.
correct.why: Collaborative review helps specialists identify duplicated work across boundaries.

correct.text: Bring the specialists together to validate the WBS.
correct.why: Group validation surfaces redundant tasks that individual interviews can miss.

distractors.text: Enlist subject matter experts for scheduling guidance.
distractors.trap: Estimating help does not reveal cross-team duplication.

distractors.text: Improve communication channels with all specialists.
distractors.trap: Too broad to directly catch redundant WBS items.

distractors.text: Prepare a task assignment matrix for the WBS.
distractors.trap: Assignments document ownership, not duplicated scope.

distractors.text: Ask each department head to approve its tasks.
distractors.trap: Separate approval can still miss overlap.

distractors.text: Compress similar tasks after the schedule is built.
distractors.trap: This fixes symptoms after planning.

distractors.text: Let the PMO standardize the WBS format.
distractors.trap: Formatting does not expose redundant work.

explanation: Reviewing the WBS in joint sessions lets specialists share approaches, reduce overlap, and build awareness, directly preventing the redundancy that individual meetings allowed.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A team is developing a new product using a hybrid approach. After the fourth sprint they learn the exchange rate for imported goods will rise sharply. What should the project manager do to ensure the final product still launches on time and as specified?

On a hybrid product initiative, the team discovers after several iterations that imported materials will become much more expensive. What should the project manager do to protect the launch date and agreed specification?
A manufacturing product project uses hybrid delivery. Midway through early sprints, the team learns currency changes will sharply increase the cost of imported components. What is the best response?

During a hybrid delivery effort, exchange-rate movement creates a significant cost increase for required imports. The date and product specification must remain intact. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Work with stakeholders to develop incremental budgeting approaches.
correct.why: Incremental budgeting gives funding flexibility as new financial information emerges.

correct.text: Collaborate with stakeholders on incremental funding options.
correct.why: Adaptive funding supports timely decisions without cutting agreed scope.

correct.text: Create an incremental budget approach with stakeholders.
correct.why: Flexible budgeting helps absorb change while preserving schedule and specification.

distractors.text: Reduce specifications to use fewer imported materials.
distractors.trap: This violates the agreed product specification.

distractors.text: Spend more now and offset it next sprint.
distractors.trap: Defers the issue without a funding model.

distractors.text: Review the backlog impact of a budget increase.
distractors.trap: Analysis alone does not adapt funding.

distractors.text: Delay the launch until exchange rates stabilize.
distractors.trap: This conflicts with the required launch date.

distractors.text: Ask procurement to remove imported components.
distractors.trap: Changes scope rather than managing budget.

distractors.text: Use management reserve without stakeholder discussion.
distractors.trap: Bypasses needed governance and alignment.

explanation: The PM should partner with stakeholders on flexible, incremental budgeting that keeps enough rigor for business needs while giving the team autonomy over funds to handle emerging change, rather than cutting scope or improvising.

eco_task: Integrate Project Planning Activities
topic: Integrate Project Planning Activities
prompts: A project in planning needs significant time for data collection and analysis. The executive sponsor asks the project manager to skip a planned focus group and brainstorming sessions to speed things up. What is the most appropriate next step?
During planning for a research-heavy project, the sponsor wants to cancel stakeholder workshops to shorten the schedule. What should the project manager do next?
A public-sector initiative depends on analysis and user input, but the sponsor asks the project manager to drop planned discovery sessions. What is the best immediate response?
The project manager has scheduled focus-group and brainstorming activities, but the executive sponsor is pressing to skip them for speed. What should happen first?

correct.text: Meet with the sponsor to understand the time pressure.
correct.why: Understanding constraints helps the PM protect quality while addressing the sponsor's concern.

correct.text: Discuss the sponsor's reasons for accelerating planning.
correct.why: The PM should understand the driver before changing stakeholder engagement activities.

correct.text: Clarify why the sponsor wants the sessions skipped.
correct.why: Exploring the rationale supports a balanced decision about speed, quality, and input.

distractors.text: Report the issue to the executive oversight board.
distractors.trap: Escalates before discussing the concern.

distractors.text: Accept the request and skip the sessions.
distractors.trap: Complies without assessing impact.

distractors.text: Tell the sponsor the project will be jeopardized.
distractors.trap: Makes a conclusion before understanding reasons.

distractors.text: Replace focus groups with an email survey immediately.
distractors.trap: Changes the approach before clarifying constraints.

distractors.text: Ask the team to work overtime on analysis.
distractors.trap: Solves schedule pressure without addressing the request.

distractors.text: Update the risk register and continue as planned.
distractors.trap: Avoids engaging the sponsor's concern.

explanation: Meeting the sponsor to understand the time constraints lets the PM weigh alternatives that keep the project on time and on budget while preserving quality; escalating, complying, or pushing back all skip that dialogue.

eco_task: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
topic: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
prompts: An organization switching from predictive to agile expects to deliver more customer value and mandates agile for all projects. What should the project manager do to ensure benefits realization?
A company is moving from waterfall delivery to agile because leaders expect stronger customer value. What should the project manager align to help realize the intended benefits?
An enterprise has required agile delivery across projects to improve value delivery. How should the project manager keep benefits realization connected to the team's work?
A software organization mandates agile practices after deciding predictive delivery is not producing enough customer value. What should the project manager do to support benefits realization?

correct.text: Align the benefits management plan with the product backlog.
correct.why: Backlog priorities should reflect targeted benefits so value is delivered early and continuously.

correct.text: Map the benefits plan to backlog priorities.
correct.why: Linking benefits to backlog order keeps delivery focused on the highest-value outcomes.

correct.text: Use the benefits plan to guide product backlog ordering.
correct.why: Benefit-driven ordering helps each iteration contribute to expected value.

distractors.text: Assess benefits realization during daily standups.
distractors.trap: Standups are not sufficient benefit governance.

distractors.text: Review benefits only at the end of each sprint.
distractors.trap: Too late to guide backlog priority.

distractors.text: Have the agile coach report benefits daily.
distractors.trap: The coach does not own benefit realization.

distractors.text: Mandate the same velocity target for all teams.
distractors.trap: Velocity does not prove benefits delivered.

distractors.text: Convert all requirements into user stories first.
distractors.trap: Format change alone does not align benefits.

distractors.text: Ask customers to approve each sprint retrospective.
distractors.trap: Retrospectives improve process, not benefit alignment.

explanation: Aligning the benefits management plan with a benefits-prioritized product backlog ensures the team delivers the most valuable features first so each sprint contributes to realization; the other options monitor too little or too late.

eco_task: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
topic: Evaluate and Deliver Project Benefits and Value
prompts: At the end of a large project, stakeholders realize the achieved benefits differ from those expected. Which item, if poorly defined at the start, could have caused this?
A major program closes and sponsors find that actual benefits do not match what they anticipated. What was likely poorly defined early in the project?
When a large transformation finishes, stakeholders cannot reconcile expected value with the benefits actually achieved. Which missing or weak definition most likely contributed?
At project completion, leaders disagree about whether the intended benefits were delivered. Which planning item was probably insufficiently defined?

correct.text: Benefits metrics
correct.why: Benefit metrics define how expected value will be measured and monitored.

correct.text: Benefit measurement criteria
correct.why: Clear measurement criteria are needed to compare planned and realized benefits.

correct.text: Benefits realization metrics
correct.why: Without metrics, stakeholders cannot reliably track whether benefits are achieved.

distractors.text: Requirements documentation
distractors.trap: Requirements define scope, not benefit measurement.

distractors.text: Project scope
distractors.trap: Scope describes deliverables, not realized value.

distractors.text: Business case
distractors.trap: It justifies investment but may not measure benefits.

distractors.text: Stakeholder register
distractors.trap: Identifies people, not benefit tracking measures.

distractors.text: Risk response plan
distractors.trap: Addresses threats, not benefit measurement.

distractors.text: Resource management plan
distractors.trap: Plans staffing, not value realization.

explanation: Detecting whether benefits are achieved requires metric monitoring throughout the life cycle; poorly defined benefits metrics leave the gap between expected and actual benefits undetected.

eco_task: Integrate Project Planning Activities
topic: Integrate Project Planning Activities
prompts: The team is about to begin work on a critical subsystem. Two technical alternatives have been flagged, but it is unclear whether either will perform adequately, and the project will be compromised if the subsystem underperforms. Which action should come first?
Before starting a vital platform component, engineers identify two possible designs but lack evidence that either will meet performance needs. What should the project manager have the team do first?
A project depends on a critical module, and two solution options are under consideration. Because poor performance would threaten the project, what is the first appropriate action?
The team must choose between two technical approaches for a key subsystem, but neither has been proven adequate. What should happen before implementation planning?

correct.text: Direct the team to evaluate the two alternatives.
correct.why: The team needs evidence about each option before choosing or planning implementation.

correct.text: Have the team assess both subsystem alternatives.
correct.why: Evaluating the alternatives creates the data needed for a sound decision.

correct.text: Evaluate the technical options before selecting one.
correct.why: Analysis must precede commitment when subsystem performance is uncertain.

distractors.text: Add subsystem performance to the issue tracker.
distractors.trap: Logging the concern does not analyze options.

distractors.text: Schedule the most promising option with contingency.
distractors.trap: Plans implementation before evaluation.

distractors.text: Advise stakeholders of the major performance risk.
distractors.trap: Communicates risk without resolving uncertainty.

distractors.text: Ask procurement to prepare contracts for both options.
distractors.trap: Commits resources before technical assessment.

distractors.text: Choose the lower-cost alternative for now.
distractors.trap: Cost alone does not prove performance adequacy.

distractors.text: Defer the decision until integration testing.
distractors.trap: Delays learning until too late.

explanation: The alternatives must be analyzed to produce data supporting the choice before any implementation is scheduled; the other options act before that analysis exists.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: A company is expanding manufacturing to a new country. The go-live date is fixed and widely communicated, but the team is unsure there is enough time to implement all product features. What should the project manager do to ensure the critical features are ready by the committed date?
A rollout to a new market has a public launch date that cannot move. The team doubts all features will fit. What should the project manager do first to protect the most important functionality?
For an overseas operations launch, the date has already been announced. The product team is uncertain whether every feature can be completed. What is the best next step?
A manufacturing expansion must go live on a committed date, but the team questions whether the full feature set is achievable. How should the project manager proceed?

correct.text: Work with the team to size an initial release slice.
correct.why: Sizing a viable slice shows what critical scope can fit the fixed date.

correct.text: Estimate the initial release slice with the team.
correct.why: The team must understand the smallest useful feature set that can be delivered.

correct.text: Define and estimate a first release slice.
correct.why: A sized release slice supports decisions about critical features within the date.

distractors.text: Add resources to raise velocity and deliver more.
distractors.trap: More people can add coordination overhead.

distractors.text: Cut operations documentation until after go-live.
distractors.trap: Removes needed work to buy time.

distractors.text: Escalate and request a new go-live date.
distractors.trap: Escalation is premature with a fixed date.

distractors.text: Commit to all features and monitor progress weekly.
distractors.trap: Monitoring does not solve capacity uncertainty.

distractors.text: Ask executives to rank every feature themselves.
distractors.trap: Bypasses team estimation and product analysis.

distractors.text: Move testing to after the launch date.
distractors.trap: Increases risk to product readiness.

explanation: Estimating the effort for a minimal set of critical features with the team provides the input needed to hit the fixed date, unlike adding resources, cutting documentation, or escalating prematurely.

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
