explanation: After confirming acceptance, the project manager should review and close all contracts to ensure obligations and payments are met, before documenting lessons learned and releasing the team.

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
prompts: On an agile project, the executive sponsor asks whether agile permits a fixed-price contract with the customer instead of time-and-materials. The customer needs a fixed-price contract to meet firm business objectives. How should the project manager respond to the sponsor?
An agile sponsor is concerned that the customer requires a fixed-price agreement for budget approval. The sponsor asks whether agile rules out that contract type. What should the project manager say?
A customer on an agile engagement needs a fixed-price contract rather than time-and-materials. The sponsor asks whether this conflicts with agile delivery. What is the best response?
During agile planning, the sponsor asks if the team must use time-and-materials because scope will evolve. The customer has a business need for fixed price. How should the project manager respond?

correct.text: Proceed with a fixed-price contract; agile allows it.
correct.why: Agile can work with multiple contract types, including fixed price.

correct.text: Use fixed price if it fits the customer's need.
correct.why: Agile does not prohibit fixed-price agreements.

correct.text: Agree that fixed price can be used on agile work.
correct.why: Contract type should support business needs and is not barred by agile.

distractors.text: Explain that agile forbids fixed-price contracts.
distractors.trap: Agile values do not ban contract types.

distractors.text: Require time-and-materials because scope may evolve.
distractors.trap: Evolving scope does not mandate one contract type.

distractors.text: Shift overruns to the customer through time-and-materials.
distractors.trap: Ignores the customer's stated business need.

distractors.text: Cancel agile delivery and use predictive work.
distractors.trap: Contract needs do not require abandoning agile.

distractors.text: Delay contracting until all backlog items are fixed.
distractors.trap: Agile can contract without locking every item.

distractors.text: Ask procurement to reject the customer's constraint.
distractors.trap: Rejects a valid business need without justification.

explanation: Agile imposes no restriction on contract type; fixed-price, shared-risk, and master service agreements can all accommodate adaptive work, so the PM can proceed as the customer needs.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: A project has been closed, and the customer care group needs to measure how satisfied the customer is with the delivered outputs. What should be done next?
After project closure, the support organization wants to understand whether the customer is satisfied with what was delivered. What is the best next action?
A customer success team inherits the outputs after closure and must gauge customer satisfaction. What should they review?
The delivery project is closed, and the operations team now needs evidence of how pleased the customer is with the results. What should be done?

correct.text: Assess the outputs and review customer feedback.
correct.why: Delivered output assessment and feedback directly measure satisfaction.

correct.text: Review the delivered results and feedback documents.
correct.why: Customer feedback tied to outputs shows satisfaction after closure.

correct.text: Evaluate outputs against the customer's feedback.
correct.why: Satisfaction is measured through delivered results and customer responses.

distractors.text: Negotiate a new contract for similar services.
distractors.trap: New work does not measure current satisfaction.

distractors.text: Schedule a closure lessons-learned meeting.
distractors.trap: Closure is already complete.

distractors.text: Audit every phase closing document.
distractors.trap: Phase documents do not measure satisfaction.

distractors.text: Reopen the project management plan.
distractors.trap: Planning artifacts are not satisfaction measures.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to declare success.
distractors.trap: Sponsor opinion is not customer feedback.

distractors.text: Update the risk register with complaints.
distractors.trap: A risk register is not a satisfaction assessment.

explanation: Satisfaction is measured by evaluating the accepted outputs against acceptance criteria and reviewing the customer's feedback documents, not by new contracts, meetings, or audits.

eco_task: Manage Project Issues
topic: Manage Project Issues
prompts: During an agile retrospective, several team members say they were confused about the iteration's progress. What should the project manager do to prevent this confusion going forward?
In a sprint retrospective, developers explain that they could not tell how much work was left during the iteration. What should the project manager encourage?
An agile team reports that progress was unclear while the iteration was underway. What action would best improve visibility next time?
At the end of an iteration, team members say current work status was hard to understand. What should the project manager coach them to use?

correct.text: Coach the team to review the iteration board.
correct.why: The board makes iteration progress visible to the team.

correct.text: Have the team use the iteration board.
correct.why: The iteration board provides transparent progress information.

correct.text: Encourage regular review of the iteration board.
correct.why: Regular board review supports visibility and self-management.

distractors.text: Prepare a daily status report.
distractors.trap: Push reports are not the agile visibility mechanism here.

distractors.text: Refer to an iteration backlog report.
distractors.trap: This is not the standard visual progress artifact.

distractors.text: Wait for the next retrospective.
distractors.trap: The issue should be addressed before the next retrospective.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor for weekly updates.
distractors.trap: Sponsor updates do not create team visibility.

distractors.text: Replace the board with email summaries.
distractors.trap: Email reduces transparent, shared visibility.

distractors.text: Escalate confusion to the steering committee.
distractors.trap: This is a team visibility practice issue.

explanation: In agile the PM coaches the team to self-manage and use the iteration board, which gives everyone real-time visibility into progress.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: A customer is worried that the current project output will not meet the stated requirements. What could the project manager do to ease the customer's concerns?
A client tells the project manager they are concerned the product being built may fall short of requirements. What action would best address the concern?
During delivery, a customer questions whether the output will satisfy what was agreed. How can the project manager best reduce the customer's concern?
A business owner is uneasy that the current deliverable may not meet expectations. What should the project manager do to realign understanding?

correct.text: Review the agreed acceptance criteria with the customer representative.
correct.why: Acceptance criteria confirm what the output must satisfy.

correct.text: Have the value representative revisit acceptance criteria.
correct.why: Rechecking agreed criteria realigns expectations before delivery.

correct.text: Validate the customer's concerns against acceptance criteria.
correct.why: Acceptance criteria provide the basis for judging requirements satisfaction.

distractors.text: Remind the customer of previously stated requirements.
distractors.trap: Restating requirements does not validate acceptance.

distractors.text: Log the concern as a project risk.
distractors.trap: This avoids direct expectation realignment.

distractors.text: Wait to discuss it at output reviews.
distractors.trap: Waiting delays a current concern.

distractors.text: Ask the team to add extra features.
distractors.trap: Extra scope may not address acceptance.

distractors.text: Escalate the concern to procurement.
distractors.trap: Procurement is unrelated to acceptance criteria.

distractors.text: Update the schedule baseline immediately.
distractors.trap: Schedule changes do not address requirement confidence.

explanation: A value representative reviewing the agreed acceptance criteria (definition of done) with the customer realigns expectations with what will be delivered, easing the concern directly.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: A technology project is in implementation when a key stakeholder requests changes. The stakeholder and the delivery team disagree about whether the changes are needed and how to make them. What is the best response?
During implementation, a business stakeholder asks for changes, but the delivery team disagrees on their need and approach. What should the project manager do first?
A stakeholder and the project team are at odds over a requested change during delivery. They disagree on both value and implementation. What is the best initial response?
On an IT project, a requested change has created disagreement between a key stakeholder and the team. How should the project manager begin resolving it?

correct.text: Meet separately with both sides to understand concerns.
correct.why: Understanding each perspective enables a balanced resolution.

correct.text: Listen to the stakeholder and team concerns first.
correct.why: Concerns must be understood before evaluating or proposing solutions.

correct.text: Discuss the issue with each party to learn their needs.
correct.why: Separate discovery helps reveal interests behind the disagreement.

distractors.text: Conduct a risk assessment immediately.
distractors.trap: Impact analysis should follow understanding of the disagreement.

distractors.text: Present pros and cons to both parties.
distractors.trap: This assumes facts before hearing concerns.

distractors.text: Make the changes without more discussion.
distractors.trap: Unilateral action ignores stakeholder and team concerns.

distractors.text: Reject the change to protect the baseline.
distractors.trap: Dismisses the request before understanding it.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to decide immediately.
distractors.trap: Escalates before facilitation and discovery.

distractors.text: Tell the team to follow stakeholder direction.
distractors.trap: Forces compliance without resolving concerns.

explanation: The PM should first understand each party's perspective by meeting them separately; only then can risk analysis or trade-off discussions lead to a mutually acceptable solution.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Procurement
topic: Plan and Manage Procurement
prompts: With workload high and resources overloaded, management decides to add new members to the stakeholder community to better manage engagement, and has just informed the project manager. Which document should the project manager revise first?
Management adds new people to the stakeholder community because the current group is overloaded. After being informed, which project document should the project manager update first?
A project gains additional stakeholder participants to improve engagement while the team is under pressure. Which document should be revised before updating communication details?
New stakeholder representatives are assigned to support an overloaded project environment. What should the project manager update first to reflect their roles?

correct.text: Project organization chart
correct.why: It shows stakeholder roles and reporting relationships first.

correct.text: The project organization chart
correct.why: New participants must be reflected in the project structure.

correct.text: Update the project organization chart.
correct.why: Roles and relationships should be defined before communication updates.

distractors.text: Stakeholder communications approach
distractors.trap: Communication details follow role definition.

distractors.text: Issue tracker
distractors.trap: It records issues, not stakeholder structure.

distractors.text: Procurement management plan
distractors.trap: New stakeholders do not necessarily alter procurement.

distractors.text: Risk response plan
distractors.trap: This is not the first stakeholder structure update.

distractors.text: Requirements traceability matrix
distractors.trap: Requirements links are unrelated to adding stakeholders.

distractors.text: Quality management plan
distractors.trap: Quality approach does not define stakeholder roles.

explanation: Because the change adds people to the stakeholder community, the project organization chart is the logical first document to update to reflect their roles and relationships.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: A company wants to compare its customer satisfaction results against industry-leading organizations in customer care. What should the project manager use to measure performance?
An operations team wants to see how its customer satisfaction scores compare with top performers in the same industry. Which technique should be used?
A project manager needs to measure service performance by comparing customer-care results to leading organizations. What tool is most appropriate?
A company is assessing whether its customer support performance matches recognized leaders. Which quality technique supports this comparison?

correct.text: Benchmarking
correct.why: Benchmarking compares performance against comparable leading organizations.

correct.text: Use benchmarking.
correct.why: It measures performance by comparison with external standards or leaders.

correct.text: Benchmark the results.
correct.why: Benchmarking provides the needed industry comparison.

distractors.text: Mind mapping
distractors.trap: Mind mapping organizes ideas, not performance comparisons.

distractors.text: Interviews
distractors.trap: Interviews gather views but lack an external benchmark.

distractors.text: Affinity diagrams
distractors.trap: Affinity diagrams group ideas, not compare performance.

distractors.text: Brainstorming
distractors.trap: Brainstorming generates ideas, not comparative measures.

distractors.text: Nominal group technique
distractors.trap: It prioritizes ideas, not industry performance.

distractors.text: Context diagramming
distractors.trap: Context diagrams show boundaries, not satisfaction benchmarks.

explanation: Benchmarking compares products, processes, and practices against leading organizations to identify best practices and measure performance, which the other techniques do not do.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Schedule
topic: Plan and Manage Schedule
prompts: Joining a project early in its implementation phase, the project manager needs to compare planned versus actual dates and gauge progress against the schedule baseline. What should the project manager use to find this information?
A project manager takes over during implementation and needs planned dates, actual dates, and schedule baseline progress. Which source should be used?
Early in execution, a new project manager wants to see whether work is tracking against the approved schedule baseline. Where should this planned-versus-actual information be found?
A manager joining an active project must compare the baseline schedule with current actuals. What tool or system is most appropriate for locating the data?

correct.text: Use the PMIS to compare schedule baselines and actuals
correct.why: The PMIS stores schedule baselines and actual performance data.

correct.text: Use the project management information system for planned-versus-actual data
correct.why: It consolidates the schedule information needed for baseline comparison.

correct.text: Check the PMIS containing schedule baselines and actual dates
correct.why: Baseline and actual dates are tracked through the PMIS.

distractors.text: Check the resource calendar for assigned resource availability dates
distractors.trap: Shows availability, not baseline-versus-actual dates.

distractors.text: Review the project charter for authorized milestone information
distractors.trap: Authorizes the project but lacks actuals.

distractors.text: Use key performance indicators as the source of schedule data
distractors.trap: KPIs are measures, not source systems.

distractors.text: Review the stakeholder register for implementation status updates
distractors.trap: Tracks stakeholders, not schedule performance.

distractors.text: Check the communications management plan for reporting cadence
distractors.trap: Defines communications, not schedule actuals.

distractors.text: Review team resource assignments to infer schedule progress
distractors.trap: Assignments do not provide baseline tracking.

explanation: The PMIS gathers, integrates, and disseminates project process outputs, making it the source for comparing planned versus actual dates against the schedule baseline.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Scope
topic: Plan and Manage Scope
prompts: An executive sponsor questions whether certain detailed capabilities in the product design align with the scope defined in the project charter. What should the project manager use to manage this?
A sponsor asks how specific product requirements connect to the objectives and scope approved in the charter. Which artifact should the project manager use?
During design review, leadership challenges whether several detailed features are within the authorized scope. What should be used to verify the link?
The project manager needs to show that each detailed requirement supports the project's approved objectives and charter scope. What tool is appropriate?

correct.text: Use a requirements traceability matrix to verify scope linkage
correct.why: It links requirements to objectives and approved scope.

correct.text: Use the requirements traceability matrix for objective alignment
correct.why: The matrix verifies each requirement against project objectives.

correct.text: Review the requirements traceability matrix for scope alignment
correct.why: Traceability shows whether detailed requirements align with scope.

distractors.text: Use expert judgment to decide whether the features belong
distractors.trap: Opinion does not create auditable links.

distractors.text: Review a prototype to see how the capabilities behave
distractors.trap: Shows design, not requirement traceability.

distractors.text: Use a context diagram to confirm product boundary interactions
distractors.trap: Shows boundaries, not objective links.

distractors.text: Review the issue log for open questions about design scope
distractors.trap: Records issues, not scope alignment.

distractors.text: Use a stakeholder engagement matrix to confirm sponsor support
distractors.trap: Tracks engagement, not requirement origin.

distractors.text: Review the resource management plan for capability ownership
distractors.trap: Does not link capabilities to scope.

explanation: The requirements traceability matrix connects each requirement to business and project objectives, letting the PM confirm which capabilities align with the charter's scope.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Schedule
topic: Plan and Manage Schedule
prompts: While developing the schedule management plan for an infrastructure upgrade, the executive sponsor asks for a schedule with zero total float. The project manager reviews candidate network paths that satisfy the schedule constraints. Which relationship between early and late dates should the project manager select?
A sponsor wants the schedule network path with zero total float for a facility upgrade. Which early and late date relationship should the project manager look for?
During schedule analysis, the project manager must identify the path where no total float exists. What relationship among early and late dates indicates this?
For a constrained infrastructure schedule, the team needs a network path with zero total float. Which condition should appear on the activities?

correct.text: Early finish equals late finish on all activities.
correct.why: Equal early and late dates indicate zero float.

correct.text: Early and late finish dates are the same throughout.
correct.why: No gap between early and late dates means no float.

correct.text: Each activity's early finish equals its late finish.
correct.why: Matching finish dates across activities show zero total float.

distractors.text: Early finish is earlier than late finish on all activities.
distractors.trap: A date gap means positive float.

distractors.text: Early finish equals late finish on some activities.
distractors.trap: Some zero-float activities do not make the path zero-float.

distractors.text: Early finish is earlier than late finish on some activities.
distractors.trap: Those activities still have float.

distractors.text: Late start is earlier than early start on all activities.
distractors.trap: That relationship is illogical for schedule calculations.

distractors.text: Early dates are ignored for constrained activities.
distractors.trap: Early and late dates are essential to float calculation.

distractors.text: Late finish exceeds early finish throughout the path.
distractors.trap: Excess late finish indicates available float.

explanation: Zero total float requires early dates to equal late dates on every activity, forcing all work to be scheduled with no slack.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: During planning, the project manager performs a cost-benefit analysis and obtains a ratio of 0.75. What does this value indicate, and how should the project manager respond?
A planning analysis shows a cost-benefit ratio of 0.75 for the proposed project. What should the project manager conclude and do next?
The project manager calculates a benefit-cost result below 1, specifically 0.75, while evaluating feasibility. What is the appropriate interpretation and response?
During business case review, the project manager finds the cost-benefit ratio is 0.75. What does this mean for the project decision?

correct.text: Costs outweigh benefits; consult the sponsor on feasibility.
correct.why: A ratio below 1 means costs exceed benefits.

correct.text: Benefits do not justify costs; involve the sponsor.
correct.why: The below-1 ratio raises a sponsor-level feasibility decision.

correct.text: Costs exceed benefits, so escalate feasibility to the sponsor.
correct.why: The analysis indicates the project may not be viable.

distractors.text: The result is inconclusive, so rerun the analysis.
distractors.trap: A value below 1 is already meaningful.

distractors.text: Expert reports should decide because the ratio is ambiguous.
distractors.trap: The ratio's basic meaning is clear.

distractors.text: Benefits outweigh costs; update the benefits plan.
distractors.trap: This reverses the below-1 interpretation.

distractors.text: The project is automatically approved for execution.
distractors.trap: A poor ratio does not justify automatic approval.

distractors.text: The team should reduce reporting frequency.
distractors.trap: Reporting changes do not address feasibility.

distractors.text: The ratio shows costs and benefits are equal.
distractors.trap: Equality would be 1, not 0.75.

explanation: A benefit-cost ratio of 0.75 conclusively means costs exceed benefits; only the sponsor who funds the project can decide whether it remains feasible.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Schedule
topic: Plan and Manage Schedule
prompts: A year into execution, a long-term project shows a schedule performance index (SPI) of 0.95 with a trend analysis indicating the SPI is declining. What is the best response?

correct.text: Review and assess the schedule to find the source of the delay.
correct.why: A mild slip warrants investigating the cause and seeking recovery before any baseline change.

distractors.text: Reallocate critical resources to other projects.
distractors.trap: Removing resources worsens a schedule already trending behind.

distractors.text: Submit a change request for the schedule baseline.
distractors.trap: Rebaselining is premature before the delay's cause is understood.

distractors.text: Use the risk breakdown structure to restructure risk categories.
distractors.trap: Reorganizing risk categories does nothing about the schedule slip.

explanation: An SPI of 0.95 is only slightly behind; the PM should first investigate the source of the delay and look for recovery opportunities before considering a rebaseline.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: While conducting product testing, the project manager flags several quality issues and wants to identify and address their root cause. What is the best response?
During acceptance testing, multiple defects appear. The project manager wants to understand the underlying causes rather than just symptoms. What should be analyzed?
A team finds recurring quality problems in a deliverable and needs to trace causes to the source. Which analysis should the project manager use?
Product testing reveals several related defects, and the project manager wants to determine why they are happening. What is the most appropriate tool?

correct.text: Analyze the results of a fishbone diagram.
correct.why: A fishbone diagram maps causes to effects for root cause analysis.

correct.text: Use a fishbone diagram for root cause analysis.
correct.why: It organizes possible causes behind a quality problem.

correct.text: Review a cause-and-effect diagram.
correct.why: Cause-and-effect diagrams help identify root causes of defects.

distractors.text: Analyze an affinity diagram.
distractors.trap: It groups ideas, not cause-and-effect chains.

distractors.text: Analyze a histogram.
distractors.trap: It shows frequency distribution, not causes.

distractors.text: Analyze a matrix diagram.
distractors.trap: It shows relationships, not root-cause structure.

distractors.text: Review the stakeholder register.
distractors.trap: Stakeholder data does not reveal defect causes.

distractors.text: Prepare a resource histogram.
distractors.trap: Resource loading does not identify quality root causes.

distractors.text: Use a communications model.
distractors.trap: Communication flow is not the defect analysis tool.

explanation: A fishbone (cause-and-effect) diagram visualizes how potential causes relate to the observed quality problems, making it the tool for root-cause analysis.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: On an $8 million project, the planned value is $954,965, the actual cost is $934,965, and the earned value is $964,965. What is the current status of the project?

correct.text: Ahead of schedule and under budget.
correct.why: EV exceeds both PV and AC, so SPI and CPI are above 1.

distractors.text: Behind schedule and over budget.
distractors.trap: Would require EV below both PV and AC.

distractors.text: Ahead of schedule and over budget.
distractors.trap: Over budget needs AC above EV, but AC is lower here.

distractors.text: Behind schedule and under budget.
distractors.trap: Behind schedule needs EV below PV, but EV is higher here.

explanation: EV ($964,965) exceeds both PV and AC, giving positive schedule and cost variances, so the project is ahead of schedule and under budget.

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
prompts: An executive sponsor gives the project charter to a newly assigned project manager. Which action should come first?
A newly named project manager receives the charter for a strategic initiative from the sponsor. What should the project manager do first?
After taking over a new project, the project manager is handed the approved charter by the sponsor. Which first step is most appropriate?
A sponsor asks a project manager to begin work after issuing the charter. Before planning details, what should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Review the business case and project goals to understand the strategic objectives.
correct.why: The manager must understand purpose and goals before detailed planning begins.

correct.text: Examine the business case and objectives to understand why the project exists.
correct.why: Initiation starts with understanding the project purpose and intended value.

correct.text: Study the charter context, business case, and goals before planning the work.
correct.why: Planning should be grounded in the project's approved purpose and goals.

distractors.text: Build the project schedule and identify the critical path.
distractors.trap: Scheduling belongs in planning after the purpose is understood.

distractors.text: Assign tasks to the team and set milestone dates.
distractors.trap: Task assignment occurs after initial understanding and planning.

distractors.text: Negotiate baseline changes with the sponsor immediately.
distractors.trap: Baselines are not set or changed before understanding intent.

distractors.text: Conduct a kickoff meeting and request status reports.
distractors.trap: Kickoff and reporting come after the project is understood.

distractors.text: Develop the risk register and response plans first.
distractors.trap: Risk planning follows clarification of project objectives.

distractors.text: Create a detailed budget using available resource estimates.
distractors.trap: Budget development is premature before reviewing business goals.

explanation: The PM must first understand the project's purpose, goals, and strategic alignment via the business case; the other options are planning activities that follow initiation.

eco_task: Integrate Project Planning Activities
topic: Integrate Project Planning Activities
prompts: A newly developed procedure could speed up the project by 25 percent, but the team notes that because it is new, there is little knowledge base or technical support. What should the project manager do to determine whether the project would benefit from using it?

On a product project, a new procedure may reduce work time by 25 percent. The team warns that support materials and technical help are limited. How should the project manager validate whether it will help?
A delivery team identifies a new work procedure that might accelerate the project by 25 percent, but experience with it is scarce. What should the project manager do before adopting it broadly?
A new procedure promises a 25 percent schedule improvement, yet the organization has little practical knowledge or support for it. What is the best way to determine its value?

correct.text: Conduct a pilot test of the new procedure.
correct.why: A pilot provides practical evidence of benefits, limits, and support needs.

correct.text: Run a limited pilot before deciding whether to adopt it.
correct.why: A controlled trial validates real performance before broader use.

correct.text: Test the procedure on a small scale first.
correct.why: Small-scale use reveals whether the procedure actually benefits the project.

distractors.text: Assess the risks and benefits using only available documentation.
distractors.trap: Analysis alone lacks hands-on evidence for a new procedure.

distractors.text: Consult external experts and follow their recommendation.
distractors.trap: Expert input helps but does not replace project-specific trial.

distractors.text: Build a full knowledge base before any use.
distractors.trap: That invests heavily before proving the procedure works.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to mandate the procedure for all work.
distractors.trap: Mandating use skips validation of value and feasibility.

distractors.text: Update the schedule immediately to reflect the faster pace.
distractors.trap: The improvement has not yet been demonstrated.

distractors.text: Reject the procedure because support is currently limited.
distractors.trap: Dismisses a potentially useful approach without testing it.

explanation: A pilot test lets the team gain hands-on experience, verify the projected benefit, and surface problems before committing the new procedure to the full project.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Scope
topic: Plan and Manage Scope
prompts: Joining the planning phase of a global project with a fixed budget and tight deadline, the project manager wants to prevent scope creep so the product ships on time and meets requirements. What is the best response?
A project manager enters planning for an international initiative with little schedule flexibility and a fixed budget. To avoid uncontrolled scope growth, what should the project manager do?
During planning for a time-constrained global product launch, the project manager wants requirements met without allowing informal additions. Which response best supports that goal?
A global project must deliver on time within an approved budget. The project manager is concerned about stakeholders adding work informally. What should be established?

correct.text: Define a scope baseline and require formal approval for changes.
correct.why: A baseline and change control prevent uncontrolled additions to scope.

correct.text: Establish the scope baseline and route changes through change control.
correct.why: Approved baselines plus formal control are the standard scope-creep defense.

correct.text: Baseline the scope and use an approval process for requested changes.
correct.why: Scope must be defined and protected through controlled change handling.

distractors.text: Review the business case to confirm economic feasibility.
distractors.trap: Feasibility review does not control later scope requests.

distractors.text: Approve each deliverable change as development progresses.
distractors.trap: Ad hoc approvals encourage unmanaged scope growth.

distractors.text: Block stakeholder contact with delivery team members.
distractors.trap: Restricting communication harms engagement without controlling changes.

distractors.text: Add schedule contingency for future stakeholder requests.
distractors.trap: Contingency alone does not authorize or control scope.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to reject all new requirements.
distractors.trap: Blanket rejection bypasses proper change evaluation.

distractors.text: Hold daily scope meetings with all interested stakeholders.
distractors.trap: Meetings do not substitute for a controlled baseline.

explanation: Scope creep is prevented by establishing a scope baseline and enforcing a formal change control process for any changes to it.

eco_task: Manage Project Issues
topic: Manage Project Issues
prompts: The project manager documents a new risk that, if not addressed immediately, would jeopardize the project's success. The resources needed to address it are assigned to another critical project. What is the best response?
A serious risk is identified on a project, and immediate response work requires specialists already committed to another high-priority project. What should the project manager do?

After logging a threat that could endanger project success, the project manager finds that the needed shared resources report to a functional manager and are busy elsewhere. What is the best next step?
A project needs scarce resources right away to respond to a major risk, but those people are assigned to another critical initiative. How should the project manager proceed?

correct.text: Negotiate a solution with the functional manager and executive sponsor.
correct.why: Shared resource conflicts require negotiation with those controlling priorities and assignments.

correct.text: Work with the functional manager and sponsor to resolve the resource conflict.
correct.why: They can align priorities and release or reassign the needed resources.

correct.text: Negotiate resource availability with the functional manager and sponsor.
correct.why: The issue is access to shared resources controlled outside the project.

distractors.text: Outsource the required resources without further discussion.
distractors.trap: External help may follow, but internal negotiation comes first.

distractors.text: Update the schedule using the pessimistic impact estimate.
distractors.trap: Schedule modeling does not secure the needed resources.

distractors.text: Ask only the sponsor for more resources and a delay.
distractors.trap: Skips the functional manager controlling the staff.

distractors.text: Accept the risk until the other project releases resources.
distractors.trap: The risk requires immediate action to protect success.

distractors.text: Reassign internal team members regardless of skill fit.
distractors.trap: Random reassignment may not address the risk effectively.

distractors.text: Escalate directly to the customer for prioritization.
distractors.trap: Customer escalation bypasses internal resource governance.

explanation: In a functional or matrix organization the functional manager reassigns shared resources, so negotiating with them and the sponsor is the way to free the resources needed to address the risk.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: While closing out a product development project, a project manager learns that a team member is still providing technical support to the customer. This support is outside the project's scope but is essential for operating the product. What should the project manager do before formally closing the project?

During closure of a software project, the project manager discovers that a specialist continues to support the customer with operational use. The work is necessary but was not included in scope. What must happen before closure?
As a manufacturing project nears formal closeout, a team member is still giving the client technical help that is required for use but is outside approved scope. What should the project manager do?
At project close, the project manager finds ongoing customer support work that is essential for the delivered product but was never authorized in the project scope. What is the best response?

correct.text: Request governance review board authorization for the additional scope.
correct.why: Out-of-scope support must be formally authorized before closure and handover.

correct.text: Have the review board formally approve the added support scope.
correct.why: Formal approval documents the necessary work before the project closes.

correct.text: Submit the added support work for governance authorization.
correct.why: The work is needed but outside scope, so approval is required.

distractors.text: Record the support need only in the lessons learned log.
distractors.trap: Lessons learned do not authorize current out-of-scope work.

distractors.text: Apply an enterprise OPM framework to absorb operations.
distractors.trap: Over-expands the issue beyond scope authorization.

distractors.text: Ask the sponsor to create a new operations plan.
distractors.trap: Over-plans instead of authorizing the specific added scope.

distractors.text: Close the project and let operations handle the support.
distractors.trap: Closure should not ignore unresolved unauthorized work.

distractors.text: Tell the team member to stop support immediately.
distractors.trap: Stopping essential support may harm product operation.

distractors.text: Update the lessons learned repository after formal closure.
distractors.trap: Documentation after closure does not resolve authorization.

explanation: The support work is necessary but outside the current scope, so the project manager must have the governance board formally authorize it, ensuring the work is documented and legitimized before handover at closure.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Procurement
topic: Plan and Manage Procurement
prompts: A project is in its final stages when a team member reports that equipment supplied by a subcontractor is inadequate for the job. Which document should the project manager review to determine how to handle this issue?
Near completion, a project team discovers that subcontractor-provided equipment does not meet the project's needs. What document should the project manager consult first?
In the closing stretch of a construction effort, a subcontracted item is reported as unsuitable for its intended work. Which document guides the project manager's response?
A supplier's equipment appears inadequate late in the project. To determine the contractual handling of the issue, what should the project manager review?

correct.text: Review the procurement contract scope document for obligations
correct.why: It defines seller obligations, specifications, and quality expectations for the item.

correct.text: Check the subcontractor's contract scope document for specifications
correct.why: The contract scope states what the supplier agreed to provide.

correct.text: Review the procurement statement of work in the contract
correct.why: It contains the binding requirements used to resolve conformance issues.

distractors.text: Review the requirements management plan for requirements handling rules
distractors.trap: Governs internal handling, not seller obligations.

distractors.text: Check the change management plan before reviewing contract conformance
distractors.trap: Change process follows conformance checking.

distractors.text: Use the stakeholder communications approach to guide the response
distractors.trap: Does not define equipment specifications.

distractors.text: Review the resource management plan for equipment assignment details
distractors.trap: Does not state subcontractor deliverable terms.

distractors.text: Check the project charter for high-level equipment expectations
distractors.trap: Authorizes project, not supplier specifications.

distractors.text: Review the risk management plan for issue escalation steps
distractors.trap: Does not determine contractual compliance.

explanation: The procurement contract scope document specifies the item's requirements and quality levels and is the agreement with the subcontractor, making it the basis for negotiating an acceptable solution.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: Delivery specialists are revising deliverables without properly documenting the revisions, telling the project manager the changes are minor. Which component of the project management plan should the project manager review with the team?
Team members are making small updates to deliverables and saying formal documentation is unnecessary. Which plan should the project manager revisit with them?
On a project, specialists keep adjusting work products without recording the updates because they consider them minor. What should the project manager review with the team?
A delivery team is informally revising deliverables and not documenting the changes. Which part of the project management plan addresses this behavior?

correct.text: Change management plan
correct.why: All revisions are changes and must follow the defined change process.

correct.text: The project's change management plan
correct.why: It explains how changes are documented, evaluated, and controlled.

correct.text: Review the change management plan with the team.
correct.why: Minor changes still require proper documentation and control.

distractors.text: Quality management plan
distractors.trap: Quality standards do not define change documentation flow.

distractors.text: Requirements management plan
distractors.trap: Requirements handling is not the core uncontrolled-change issue.

distractors.text: Scope management plan
distractors.trap: Scope management helps, but revision control is the issue.

distractors.text: Communications management plan
distractors.trap: Communication rules do not authorize undocumented revisions.

distractors.text: Configuration management database
distractors.trap: A repository alone does not define the change process.

distractors.text: Work performance data
distractors.trap: Performance data reports progress, not change governance.

explanation: Revisions are changes, so the change management plan governs how they must be evaluated and documented, and it indicates which related documents also need updating.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: A project manager discovers a difference between a product's planned performance rate and its actual rate, a metric that was not defined in the project management plan. The project manager must determine whether any action is needed. In which process group does this determination occur?
A product's actual performance rate differs from the planned rate, and the project manager needs to decide whether corrective action is required. Which process group covers this determination?
During delivery, a project manager finds a variance between expected and actual product performance. Where is the decision made about whether action is necessary?
A project manager compares planned product performance with actual results and must decide if the variance requires action. Which process group is involved?

correct.text: Use the Monitoring and Controlling process group
correct.why: This group compares planned and actual results to decide on needed action.

correct.text: Monitoring and Controlling, where performance variances are evaluated
correct.why: Control work determines whether variances require corrective or preventive action.

correct.text: The process group for monitoring and controlling project work
correct.why: It tracks performance against plans and determines appropriate responses.

distractors.text: Use the Closing process group to verify final acceptance
distractors.trap: Closing formally ends accepted work.

distractors.text: Use the Initiating process group to authorize the project
distractors.trap: Initiating happens before performance data exists.

distractors.text: Use the Executing process group to carry out the work
distractors.trap: Executing performs work; control evaluates results.

distractors.text: Use the Planning process group to define the project approach
distractors.trap: Planning does not handle ongoing variance response.

distractors.text: Use the project start-up process group for authorization decisions
distractors.trap: Start-up authorization is not performance control.

distractors.text: Use administrative closure to document completed project records
distractors.trap: Administrative closure does not manage active variances.

explanation: Comparing planned against actual results to decide whether corrective action is needed is the defining activity of the Monitoring and Controlling process group.

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
prompts: A project manager wants to compare the effectiveness of corrective actions taken on the current project against those taken on a similar project to generate improvement ideas. What should the project manager use?
To improve current corrective actions, a project manager wants to compare results with actions used on a comparable past project. Which technique is appropriate?
A project manager is looking for improvement ideas by comparing current project corrective-action results with a similar project's results. What should be used?
The team wants to learn whether its corrective actions are as effective as those from another comparable project. Which tool or technique fits best?

correct.text: Benchmarking
correct.why: Benchmarking compares performance with comparable work to identify improvements.

correct.text: Use benchmarking
correct.why: It evaluates metrics against similar projects or organizations for improvement ideas.

correct.text: Apply a benchmarking comparison
correct.why: The need is cross-project comparison of effectiveness and practices.

distractors.text: Process analysis
distractors.trap: It examines process steps without requiring external comparison.

distractors.text: Expert judgment
distractors.trap: Experts advise, but do not provide the comparison itself.

distractors.text: Quality audits
distractors.trap: Audits verify compliance, not comparative effectiveness.

distractors.text: Root cause analysis
distractors.trap: It finds causes rather than comparing with another project.

distractors.text: Design of experiments
distractors.trap: It tests variables, not prior corrective-action comparisons.

distractors.text: Affinity grouping
distractors.trap: It organizes ideas but does not benchmark performance.

explanation: Benchmarking compares planned or actual metrics against those of comparable projects, which is exactly the cross-project comparison needed to generate improvement ideas.

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
prompts: A project team hit a key milestone by meeting critical technical requirements on a new process, then used that process to build the first of several similar deliverables. Why should the project manager document this in the lessons learned repository?
A team successfully used a new process to meet important technical requirements and produce the first item in a series of similar deliverables. Why capture this lesson?
After a new process helps the project meet a critical milestone, the team begins applying it to repeated deliverables. Why should this be added to lessons learned?
A project develops a process that satisfies key technical needs and appears useful for similar future work. What is the main reason to document it?

correct.text: The new process produced benefits that could apply to future projects.
correct.why: Lessons learned capture reusable knowledge so future projects can benefit.

correct.text: The process created reusable knowledge for similar future work.
correct.why: The repository preserves useful practices for later teams and projects.

correct.text: Future projects may benefit from the successful process.
correct.why: Lessons learned are intended to transfer proven knowledge forward.

distractors.text: The information can support organizational performance appraisals.
distractors.trap: Appraisals are not the purpose of lessons learned.

distractors.text: The repository can replace training for similar deliverables.
distractors.trap: Lessons may support training but do not replace it.

distractors.text: The costs should be captured to update the budget.
distractors.trap: Budget tracking is separate from lessons learned.

distractors.text: The milestone should be used to close the project early.
distractors.trap: A milestone success alone does not justify closure.

distractors.text: The process should become mandatory across all projects.
distractors.trap: Documentation does not automatically mandate enterprise use.

distractors.text: The team should avoid revisiting the process later.
distractors.trap: Lessons learned encourage future review and reuse.

explanation: The primary purpose of the lessons learned repository is to preserve knowledge, in this case the new process's benefits, so future projects and phases can leverage it.

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
prompts: A team member has completed all assigned tasks, and the functional manager now asks the project manager to reassign that person to a similar project. What is the best response?
A specialist finishes their project work, and the functional manager wants to move the specialist to another similar initiative. What should the project manager do first?
After completing all assigned work, a team member is requested for reassignment to a comparable project. What is the project manager's best response?
A functional manager asks to release a team member whose tasks are complete so the person can support another project. What should happen before reassignment?

correct.text: Direct the team member to update the lessons learned log.
correct.why: Fresh knowledge should be captured before the resource leaves the project.

correct.text: Have the team member record relevant lessons before release.
correct.why: Capturing lessons now preserves useful knowledge for this and future work.

correct.text: Ask the team member to update lessons learned first.
correct.why: Lessons are most accurate when documented before reassignment.

distractors.text: Ask the functional manager for the next project's checklist.
distractors.trap: The next project's task list is outside this PM's role.

distractors.text: Require the team member to train a replacement.
distractors.trap: Tasks are complete, so replacement training is not central.

distractors.text: Identify another employee for the functional manager.
distractors.trap: Staffing the other project is not this PM's decision.

distractors.text: Refuse the reassignment until project closure.
distractors.trap: Holding completed resources unnecessarily is poor resource management.

distractors.text: Update the project charter with the reassignment.
distractors.trap: Charters do not manage routine resource release.

distractors.text: Escalate the request to the executive sponsor.
distractors.trap: Escalation is unnecessary when lessons capture can resolve it.

explanation: Before the resource moves on, the project manager should capture lessons learned while the information is fresh so improvements can be applied sooner.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: The project management plan requires that the count of occurrences per defect type be documented and displayed graphically. What should the project manager create to meet this requirement?
A quality plan calls for a graphical display showing how many times each defect category occurs. Which tool should the project manager use?
The team must present defect occurrence counts by type in a visual format. What should be created?
A project manager needs to show the frequency of each defect type as part of quality reporting. Which chart is appropriate?

correct.text: Histogram
correct.why: A histogram displays frequency counts across categories or ranges.

correct.text: Create a histogram
correct.why: It graphically shows numerical counts, such as defects by type.

correct.text: Use a histogram chart
correct.why: Histograms are suited for displaying defect occurrence frequencies.

distractors.text: Cause-and-effect diagram
distractors.trap: It explores root causes, not frequency counts.

distractors.text: Affinity diagram
distractors.trap: It groups ideas rather than displaying numeric counts.

distractors.text: Design for X guidelines
distractors.trap: DfX guides design decisions, not defect reporting.

distractors.text: Scatter diagram
distractors.trap: It shows correlation between two variables.

distractors.text: Flowchart
distractors.trap: It maps process steps, not category frequencies.

distractors.text: Checklist
distractors.trap: It verifies items but does not graph counts.

explanation: A histogram provides a graphical representation of numerical data like the number of defects per type, which is exactly what the requirement calls for.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: A team produced 100 products per the project charter. The project manager tasks the team with evaluating all 100 against the quality metrics to identify defects. What is the team performing?
A project team completed 100 units and must check every unit against defined quality metrics to find defects. What activity is this?
The project manager asks the team to examine all 100 completed products against documented quality criteria. What technique is being used?
Every product created under the charter is being compared with quality metrics to identify nonconformance. What is the team doing?

correct.text: Inspection
correct.why: Inspection examines work products against standards to identify defects.

correct.text: Performing inspection
correct.why: Checking completed products against quality metrics is inspection.

correct.text: Conducting an inspection
correct.why: Inspection verifies deliverables or products against documented requirements.

distractors.text: Statistical sampling
distractors.trap: Sampling checks a subset, not every product.

distractors.text: Requirements traceability
distractors.trap: Traceability links requirements, not defect-checks all products.

distractors.text: Quality function deployment
distractors.trap: QFD translates needs into requirements during planning.

distractors.text: Benchmarking
distractors.trap: Benchmarking compares against external or comparable practices.

distractors.text: Process analysis
distractors.trap: Process analysis reviews workflow, not each product.

distractors.text: Design review
distractors.trap: A design review does not inspect every finished unit.

explanation: Inspection is the examination of work products to determine conformance to documented standards, which is what evaluating all 100 products against quality metrics represents.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Procurement
topic: Plan and Manage Procurement
prompts: A customer finds defects in a product, and the project manager determines that parts from a supplier failed to meet quality standards. The supplier refuses to provide replacement parts. What is the best response?
Defective customer-facing products are traced to supplier parts that do not meet required quality levels. The supplier will not replace them. What should the project manager do?
A supplier refuses to replace components that failed agreed quality standards and caused product defects. Which response is most appropriate?
After a customer reports defects, the project manager confirms a supplier's parts are nonconforming. The supplier declines replacement. What should the project manager review?

correct.text: Review the procurement management plan and the supplier contract.
correct.why: The plan and contract define obligations, remedies, and escalation steps.

correct.text: Check the procurement plan and contract with the supplier.
correct.why: Contract terms determine how to address nonconforming supplier parts.

correct.text: Review the contract and procurement management plan for remedies.
correct.why: The contract is the binding source for supplier quality obligations.

distractors.text: Submit a change request to revise the procurement SOW.
distractors.trap: Changing scope does not enforce existing quality obligations.

distractors.text: Find a new supplier and change the resource plan.
distractors.trap: Replacement is premature before using contractual remedies.

distractors.text: Escalate the issue directly to the executive sponsor.
distractors.trap: Escalation is premature before reviewing the contract.

distractors.text: Accept the defects and update the lessons learned log.
distractors.trap: Lessons learned do not resolve supplier nonconformance.

distractors.text: Ask the customer to approve lower quality parts.
distractors.trap: Customer approval does not address supplier responsibility.

distractors.text: Issue a team performance warning to procurement staff.
distractors.trap: The issue is supplier conformance, not team performance.

explanation: A contract is a mutually binding agreement obligating the seller to provide the specified products; reviewing the procurement plan and contract identifies the seller's obligations and available remedies.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Procurement
topic: Plan and Manage Procurement
prompts: A project needs support from a new supplier to complete a phase that is over budget and schedule. The project manager wants the option to expand the supplier's scope if work goes well, needs the supplier's rates fixed for the duration, and wants the supplier to be able to earn a bonus for exceeding goals. Which contract type best fits?
A supplier may be added to help a troubled project phase. The buyer wants fixed rates, possible scope expansion, and a bonus if the supplier exceeds targets. Which contract type is most suitable?
For a delayed and over-budget phase, the project manager needs a supplier contract with fixed rates and performance incentives. The work may grow if results are strong. What type fits best?
A project manager wants supplier pricing certainty while allowing incentive compensation for superior performance and possible additional work. Which procurement contract should be used?

correct.text: Fixed price incentive fee (FPIF)
correct.why: FPIF fixes price/rates while allowing incentives for meeting performance targets.

correct.text: Use a fixed price incentive fee contract
correct.why: It balances cost certainty with a bonus for exceeding defined goals.

correct.text: A fixed price incentive fee arrangement
correct.why: The seller has fixed pricing and can earn incentive compensation.

distractors.text: Cost plus award fee (CPAF)
distractors.trap: Costs are reimbursed and award fees are subjective.

distractors.text: Cost plus incentive fee (CPIF)
distractors.trap: Cost reimbursable contracts do not provide fixed price certainty.

distractors.text: Firm fixed price (FFP)
distractors.trap: FFP lacks an incentive bonus for exceeding goals.

distractors.text: Time and materials (T&M)
distractors.trap: T&M can grow in cost without fixed price certainty.

distractors.text: Cost plus fixed fee (CPFF)
distractors.trap: CPFF reimburses costs and pays a fixed fee.

distractors.text: Indefinite delivery contract
distractors.trap: It handles ordering flexibility, not fixed rates plus incentives.

explanation: FPIF sets a fixed price with fixed rates and lets the seller earn an additional amount for meeting defined performance criteria, matching the requirement for stable rates plus a performance bonus.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: A project is 75 percent complete with a schedule performance index (SPI) of 1.03 and a cost performance index (CPI) of 0.9. What is the most appropriate next step?

correct.text: Create a bottom-up estimate for the remaining work.
correct.why: A detailed estimate of remaining work packages proactively clarifies the true cost to complete.

distractors.text: Identify parallel activities to reduce the critical path.
distractors.trap: Schedule is ahead (SPI 1.03); fast-tracking adds cost and rework.

distractors.text: Determine if the contingency reserve is sufficient.
distractors.trap: Reserves are a last resort, not the first response to a cost overrun.

distractors.text: Prepare a change request for the project budget.
distractors.trap: Requesting more budget before analyzing the remaining cost is premature.

explanation: With schedule fine but cost overrunning, the project manager should proactively estimate remaining costs bottom-up before considering reserves or budget changes.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: A project to expand a municipal building is on schedule and within budget. During foundation excavation, the project manager learns the addition would cover the ruins of a historical site, so construction must move to a different location. Which action should come first?
During excavation for a city facility expansion, the team discovers protected historical remains where the addition was planned. The work must relocate. What should the project manager do first?
A public-sector building project is progressing as planned until construction would disturb a historical site, requiring a different location. Which first action is appropriate?
A facility expansion can no longer continue at the planned site because it would affect historical ruins. Before moving work, what should the project manager do?

correct.text: Assess the change impact and develop a revised project plan.
correct.why: Scope, schedule, cost, and feasibility impacts must be understood first.

correct.text: Evaluate the impact of relocation before revising the plan.
correct.why: The project manager needs impact analysis before committing to actions.

correct.text: Analyze the impact and prepare an updated project plan.
correct.why: Major site changes require assessment before permits or execution changes.

distractors.text: Negotiate with the historical site manager to continue building.
distractors.trap: This ignores the requirement to protect the site.

distractors.text: Obtain permits for the new location immediately.
distractors.trap: Permits come after impact and feasibility are assessed.

distractors.text: Move construction and hire additional resources right away.
distractors.trap: Acts before analyzing cost, schedule, and feasibility.

distractors.text: Close the project and start a new municipal project.
distractors.trap: Closure is premature before evaluating change impact.

distractors.text: Use remaining contingency to fund the relocation.
distractors.trap: Budget decisions require prior impact assessment.

distractors.text: Ask the team to continue work outside the affected area.
distractors.trap: Continuing work may be unsafe without revised planning.

explanation: The project manager must first assess the change's impact on scope, schedule, and budget, then develop a revised plan, before pursuing permits or relocation.

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
prompts: A project manager is assigned to a complex project with many milestones, numerous simultaneous tasks, and no schedule float. Management is watching closely and plans to use it as a model for future projects, so an accurate lessons learned log is essential. What is the best response?
A high-visibility project has many parallel activities, tight milestones, and no float. Leadership wants future projects to learn from it. How should the project manager handle lessons learned?

Management wants a complex, closely monitored project to become a reference for later work. Because the schedule has no slack, accurate lessons capture is important. What should the project manager do?
A project with many concurrent tasks and tight deadlines will be used as an organizational model. What is the best way to maintain its lessons learned?

correct.text: Create the lessons learned log early and update it regularly.
correct.why: Lessons should be captured continuously while information is accurate.

correct.text: Start the lessons learned log now and maintain it throughout.
correct.why: Ongoing capture prevents loss or distortion of project knowledge.

correct.text: Establish the lessons log early and keep it current.
correct.why: Continuous updates support accurate knowledge transfer to future projects.

distractors.text: Invite key stakeholders to create the log for the team.
distractors.trap: Delegating ownership delays capture by those doing the work.

distractors.text: Have knowledge transfer managers update the repository later.
distractors.trap: Later intermediaries may miss timely project learning.

distractors.text: Create the log in the charter and update only resolved issues.
distractors.trap: Lessons learned are not limited to resolved issues.

distractors.text: Wait until closure to avoid distracting the team.
distractors.trap: Waiting risks inaccurate or forgotten lessons.

distractors.text: Capture only negative events in the issue log.
distractors.trap: Lessons include successes and are not issue logs.

distractors.text: Ask management to document lessons after project completion.
distractors.trap: Management observation does not replace team learning capture.

explanation: Lessons learned is an ongoing process performed throughout the project, so creating the log early and updating it regularly best ensures accurate, reusable knowledge.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: A company needs changes to its retail portal in response to a recent market shift. Delivering the full feature set would take at least 18 months. What approach should the project manager use to pursue early benefit realization?

correct.text: Assess business value and execution difficulty, then prioritize high-value, low-effort features first.
correct.why: Sequencing high-value, low-cost work delivers business value soonest.

distractors.text: Have the team build a business case to grow staff size and compress the release cycle.
distractors.trap: Adding staff does not by itself accelerate value delivery and may delay it.

distractors.text: Use a distributed team across time zones to shorten the timeline.
distractors.trap: Around-the-clock work adds coordination overhead, not guaranteed early value.

distractors.text: Create a governance board with key stakeholders and work the critical path to align with business needs.
distractors.trap: Adds governance structure rather than sequencing value delivery.

explanation: Early benefit realization comes from assessing value versus effort and prioritizing high-value, low-execution-cost features so the most business value is delivered first.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: A company with high maturity in predictive projects has a portfolio office piloting a few iterative tools in its framework. A project manager is asked to use these tools on a current project that has a limited budget. Which action should come first?
An organization experienced with predictive delivery is experimenting with iterative tools. A project manager is asked to apply them on a budget-constrained project. What should happen first?
A portfolio office suggests using new iterative practices on a current project, but the project has limited funding and the organization is mostly predictive. What is the first step?
A mature predictive organization wants a project manager to try iterative tools on a project with tight budget constraints. Which action should the project manager take first?

correct.text: Assess whether the iterative tools are feasible within the budget.
correct.why: Fit and feasibility must be confirmed before adopting new tools.

correct.text: Evaluate budget feasibility before using the iterative tools.
correct.why: The project manager must understand constraint impacts before adoption.

correct.text: Determine if the tools can be used within project constraints.
correct.why: Budget and project fit should be assessed before implementation.

distractors.text: Train the team immediately on the iterative tools.
distractors.trap: Training is premature before confirming feasibility.

distractors.text: Obtain stakeholder approval to use the tools first.
distractors.trap: Approval should follow feasibility and fit assessment.

distractors.text: Start using the tools on the current work immediately.
distractors.trap: Immediate adoption risks budget and planning impacts.

distractors.text: Replace the predictive framework with an agile approach.
distractors.trap: A wholesale methodology shift exceeds the scenario need.

distractors.text: Ask the portfolio office to fund all project overruns.
distractors.trap: Funding assumptions do not replace feasibility analysis.

distractors.text: Document the tools in lessons learned after delivery.
distractors.trap: Lessons learned later do not guide adoption now.

explanation: The project manager must first assess whether the iterative tools are feasible within the project's limited budget and constraints, then adjust the plan before training or approval.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: After reviewing the project management plan with key stakeholders, the project manager is told the 18-month release plan is unacceptable and the product must launch in six months. Which action should come first?
Stakeholders reject an 18-month launch plan and require delivery in six months instead. Before responding, what should the project manager do first?
During plan review, key stakeholders insist that the product release be shortened from 18 months to six months. Which first step is best?
A project manager is asked to compress a release timeline from 18 months to six months. What should be done before committing to changes?

correct.text: Analyze the impact on scope, budget, resources, and schedule.
correct.why: The effects of compression must be understood before committing.

correct.text: Evaluate how the shorter timeline affects project constraints.
correct.why: Impact analysis comes before changing commitments or plans.

correct.text: Assess the shortened timeline's impact on the project plan.
correct.why: Scope, cost, resource, and risk effects must be evaluated first.

distractors.text: Renegotiate scope with stakeholders to reduce the work.
distractors.trap: Scope changes should follow impact analysis.

distractors.text: Hire additional resources to finish within six months.
distractors.trap: Adds cost before understanding the impact.

distractors.text: Ask stakeholders to agree to a more realistic date.
distractors.trap: Negotiation should be informed by impact analysis.

distractors.text: Update the baseline to reflect the six-month launch.
distractors.trap: Baselines should not change before analysis and approval.

distractors.text: Begin fast tracking all remaining project activities.
distractors.trap: Fast tracking may increase risk and needs evaluation.

distractors.text: Tell the team to work overtime until launch.
distractors.trap: Overtime decisions require impact and feasibility analysis.

explanation: The project manager should first assess how the six-month target affects scope, budget, and resources, then make informed adjustments before negotiating or adding resources.

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
prompts: Two weeks into a project, the project manager notices that some tasks were duplicated and a core task was missed. Investigating, the project manager finds that several delivery specialists were unclear about their responsibilities. What should the project manager do?
Early in a project, duplicated work and a missed key task are traced to team members not understanding who owns which responsibilities. What should the project manager do?
A project manager discovers overlapping assignments and one unassigned critical task. Team interviews show confusion about responsibilities. Which action is best?
Several specialists are unsure of their roles, resulting in duplicate tasks and missed work. How should the project manager address the problem?

correct.text: Review and clarify roles and responsibilities with the team.
correct.why: Clear ownership prevents duplicated effort and missed work.

correct.text: Clarify team roles, responsibilities, and accountability.
correct.why: Role clarity addresses the root cause of overlap and gaps.

correct.text: Review the responsibility assignments with the delivery team.
correct.why: Responsibility clarification aligns work ownership across the team.

distractors.text: Inform the sponsor and wait for direction.
distractors.trap: The PM should resolve this internal team issue.

distractors.text: Reassign all tasks to senior delivery specialists.
distractors.trap: Reassignment ignores the role-clarity root cause.

distractors.text: Increase team meetings to review task status.
distractors.trap: More status meetings do not clarify ownership.

distractors.text: Ask the customer to prioritize the missed work.
distractors.trap: Customer prioritization does not resolve team responsibility confusion.

distractors.text: Update the risk register with the duplicated tasks.
distractors.trap: Risk logging alone does not correct accountability gaps.

distractors.text: Remove the missed task from the project scope.
distractors.trap: Deleting work avoids rather than solves the issue.

explanation: Duplicated and missed work stem from unclear responsibilities, so the project manager should proactively review and clarify roles with the team, ideally using a RACI matrix.

eco_task: Manage Project Issues
topic: Manage Project Issues
prompts: Before implementing a system upgrade, a team member discovers a critical defect in the current system that must be fixed. What is the best response?

A product team is preparing to deploy a planned system upgrade when an analyst finds a severe defect in the existing system. The issue must be corrected before proceeding. What should the project manager do?
Just ahead of a technology upgrade, a developer identifies a critical problem in the current environment that requires correction. How should the project manager respond?
While readying an operational platform for its scheduled upgrade, the team uncovers a serious existing defect that cannot be ignored. What is the most appropriate next step?

correct.text: Inform the executive sponsor and use integrated change control.
correct.why: A critical defect affecting delivery requires formal evaluation and approval through integrated change control.

correct.text: Notify the sponsor and follow the Perform Integrated Change Control process.
correct.why: The defect may affect baselines, so it must be handled through formal change control.

correct.text: Escalate to the sponsor and process the fix through integrated change control.
correct.why: Sponsor awareness and integrated review are needed before changing the approved project work.

distractors.text: Install the fix together with the scheduled upgrade.
distractors.trap: Bypasses formal approval for a critical change.

distractors.text: Ask operations to resolve it outside the project.
distractors.trap: Deflects an issue that affects project delivery.

distractors.text: Wait because the scope baseline has already been approved.
distractors.trap: Ignores a critical product defect.

distractors.text: Add the defect to the issue log and continue deployment.
distractors.trap: Logging alone does not authorize corrective change.

distractors.text: Have the team correct it under normal quality activities.
distractors.trap: Treats a baseline-impacting defect as routine work.

distractors.text: Ask the customer to accept the defect until after launch.
distractors.trap: Seeks acceptance before proper impact analysis.

explanation: A critical defect with no workaround requires formal handling, so the project manager informs the sponsor and runs the Perform Integrated Change Control process.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Procurement
topic: Plan and Manage Procurement
